ISBN 978-0-470-87821-7. SCHERMERHORN, John R. Exploring management. Hoboken: Wiley, 2018. ISBN 978-1-119-39577-5.

course_title: Quality, Environment, Health and Safety Management
university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The goal of the course is to teach students he fundamental views on quality of product, connections between customer requirements and product characteristics, environmental and occupational health and safety aspects of activities of an organization both from the view of legislation and international standards. Another goal is to introduce the practical application of selected requirements of international management system standards (internal audits, document control etc.).
course_content: Concepts of quality, environment and occupational health and safety Ways of implementation of quality, environment and occupational health and safety management systems Legal requirements regarding quality, environment and occupational health and safety Requirements of quality, environment and occupational health and safety management systems Methods used in the field of quality, environment and occupational health and safety Chosen specific of quality, environment and occupational health and safety management systems (documentation and document control, internal audits, corrective nad preventive actions and ongoing improvement) System approach and management system integration
learning_outcomes: students will be able to: - name the approaches of quality management, environmental management and OHS management; - name the basic legislative acts in the field of quality, environment and OHS; - explain the content of the eight basic principles of quality management; - describe the seven simple quality management tools and explain how to use them; - explain the benefits of quality management, environment management and OHS management, including the benefits of integrating management systems; - apply the seven simple quality management tools to real problems; - propose a template for control of documentation; - identify the processes in the organization and develop maps of identified processes and map of processes (system approach); - apply simple approaches to the identification and assessment of environmental aspects and OHS risks; - identify the causes of simple problems in the quality management systems, environmental management systems and OHS management systems and propose corrective actions.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 25 %: 25 % Presentation or defence 25 %: 25 % Final test 50 %: 50 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: MALMBERG, Lasse; MOISIO, Jussi. Deploying the integrated management system : quality, environment, and health and safety ISO 9001, ISO 14001, OHSAS 18001 : self-assessment work book : 59 probing questions and contrasting pairs of examples : what separates the successful from the average?. [Turku]: Benchmarking Ltd., 2008. ISBN 978-952-5672-76-3. Recommended: Cracking the case of ISO 9001:2008 for manufacturing : a simple guide to implementing quality management in manufacturing. Milwaukee, Wisconsin: ASQ Quality Press, 2009. ISBN 9780873897617. Cracking the case of ISO 9001:2008 for service : a simple guide to implementing quality management in service organizations. Milwaukee, Wisconsin: ASQ Quality Press, 2009. ISBN 9780873897624. ISO 14000 environmental management standards [elektronický zdroj] : engineering and financial aspects. Chichester, England: Wiley, 2004. Risk-reduction methods for occupational safety and health [elektronický zdroj]. Hoboken, N.J.: Wiley, 2012.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is designed to understand the foundations of the ethical issues of the enterprise in the frame of society: Development of ethical awareness and the ability to recognize the fundamental ethical dilemmas and criteria in business practice. Explanation and understanding of ethical thinking in the historical development Basic ethical schools as orientation support for ethical decisions.
course_content: 1. Challenge of Business Ethics today Current ethical challenges of capitalist society Current challenge for universities of economics and finance 2. Challenge of economic history Personal managerial responsibility Long-term managerial responsibility Social and environmental responsibility 3. Historical development of Business ethics Development of ethical thinking in the history utill modernism Improtant influences for modern capitalism 4. The role of managerial personality Basic structure of the personality (Freud) Changes of personality structure after Adler and Jung (Frankl) Questionable subconscious power Consequences for ethical decision-making process of managers 5. Challenge of conscience Historical understanding of human conscience (heteronomy/autonomy) Limits of rationalism /authoritarian style of management Limits of empiristicism Limits of romantic feelings for ethical perspective 6. Ethics as a science Universal frame of science Position of economics as a social science Empiristic research and non-empiristic interpretation or valuation (Weber, Myrdal) 7. Ethical challenges of autonomous systems Historical roots of mechanistic approach Advantages and disadvantages of Artificial Intelligence Limits of neuroscience for moral orientation 8. Utilitaristic school Definition and examples Advantages and disadvantages of utilitarianism for economy 9. Deontological school Definition and examples Advantages and disadvantages of deontology 10. Crises of capitalism Loss of original spirit of capitalism Looking for new Spirit of capitalism 11. Symptoms of capitalism today Cases: Privatization process, Fukushima, Challenger, Bank scandals, Car industry, Food industry, Textile industry etc. 12. Virtue ethics Definition of virtue Possibility to develop the virtues Role of codes of ethics and compliance 13. Choice of offered ethical situations Ethics and human resources Ethics in the sales and marketing Ethics and accounting Ethics and financial markets (capital markets, banks and insurance companies) Ethics and Auditing Ethical code in company Ethical and social audit in company. After this lecture, students will be able to: • identify the ethical dilemmas in management according special departments or situation • defend the own personal ethical position • use the right argumentation in ethical discussion • distinguish the legal and ethical area
learning_outcomes: be able to: - clarify the basic of ethical thinking, - describe the main ethical theories, - describe the development of business ethics as an scientific discipline, - identify ethical problems or dilemmas related to the executive management dicisions, - evaluate the business strategy in terms of the ethical dimension, - implement ethical principles into the management.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 10 %: 10 % Term paper 30 %: 30 % Mid-term test(s) 20 %: 0 % Final test 20 %: 30 % Final oral exam 20 %: 30 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: COLLINS, Denis. Business ethics. Hoboken: John Wiley & Sons, 2012. ISBN 978-0-470-63994-8. SANDEL, Michael J. What money can't buy : the moral limits of markets. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2012. ISBN 978-0-374-20303-0. Globalisation, governance and ethics [elektronický zdroj] : new managerial and economic insights. New York: Nova Science Publishers, 2011. COLLINS, Denis. Behaving badly : ethical lessons from Enron. Indianapolis: Dog Ear Publishing, 2006. ISBN 1-59858-160-0. COVEY, Stephen R. The 7 habits of highly effective people : restoring the character ethic. New York: Free Press, 2004. ISBN 0-7432-6951-9.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to teach students how to rationally choose to use a tool for analyzing and managing common situations in corporate financial management. The course focuses on financial management from operational to strategic decision making.
course_content: 1. Financial management and financial objectives. 2. Time value of money and its application in financial management. 3. Risk, its concept, types, methods of measurement and impact on financial decision-making. 4. The effect of taxation on financial decisions. 5. Relations of corporate finance to financial market. Valuation of shares and bonds. 6. Financial analysis. 7. Short-term financial management. Inventory management, accounts receivable and cash management. Short-term financing alternatives. 8. Long-term financial management. Investment decisions. Financial management structure. Long-term financing alternatives. 9. Financial planning, principles, coordination and methods. 10. Methods for firms valuation. 11. The financial aspects of the M&A.
learning_outcomes: be able to understand the tasks of financial management, divide the objectives, instruments and effects on short and long term, understand the nature and impact of risks and uncertainties, handle the calculation of risk indicators, and use the results to practical decisions cope with the principle of the time value of money and decision-making based on it, tools to manage investment decisions and apply it to practice, describe the main components of financial structure, discuss problems of optimal financial structure and the various perspectives on it, prepare financial analysis for various stakeholders, circuits and describe ways of working capital - inventory, cash and receivables, analyze the main problems of financial planning, use basic valuation techniques, compare the advantages and disadvantages of mergers and acquisitions.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 20 %: 20 % Mid-term test(s) 20 %: 20 % Final test 60 %: 60 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Recommended: Allen, F., Brealey, R. A., & Myers, S. C. (2011). Principles of corporate finance. McGraw-Hill. Kaplan Publishing. Financial Management. Berkshire: Kaplan Financial Limited, 2014. ISBN: 978-1-78415-035-8.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to analyze accounting information from managerial purposes and to focus on the quantification of costs and expenses. Furthermore there is dealt with budgeting issues and investment planning.
course_content: 1. Relation between Financial Accounting, Managerial Accounting and Financial Management 2. Cost terms - concepts and performance 3. Profit Analysis. Break-Even Point. Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis. 4. Costing 5. Budgeting 6. Investment Decisions Evaluation
learning_outcomes: - explain the differences in substance and management accounting system design for product costing and compare it with the accounting information for incentives of managers at different levels of management; - provide breakeven analysis and cost - volume analysis - select the appropriate structure of product costs and benefits depending on decision-making tasks; - select appropriate methods for investment planning analysis under conditions of risk and uncertainty.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 20 %: 0 % Mid-term test(s) 20 %: 0 % Final test 60 %: 0 % Total 100 %: 0 %

Cost accounting : a managerial emphasis. Pearson Prentice Hall. Horngren, C. T. (2007). Cost accounting : a managerial emphasis : student's solutions manual. Pearson Prentice Hall. Garrison, R. H., & Noreen, E. W. (2000). Managerial accounting. Irwin/McGraw-Hill.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces fundamental conceptual and theoretical principles upon which the practice of marketing is based. It is focused on definition of marketing, analysis of marketing environment, forms of marketing research, segmentation and targeting, brand and brand positioning, marketing mix and on marketing planning. All principles are applied to diverse sectors - to consumer goods and services, B2B markets, non-profit organizations etc.
course_content: 1. Marketing, marketing concept and market orientation 2. Marketing micro environment and macro environment 3. Marketing research 4. Segmentation and targeting 5. Brand and brand positioning 6. Marketing mix: product 7. Marketing mix: price 8. Marketing mix: communication 9. Marketing mix: convenience 10.Marketing planning 11.The role of marketing in companies
learning_outcomes: - explain the fundamental marketing principles; - characterize the basic marketing strategies, models, methods, concepts and tools; - apply basic marketing strategies, methods, and tools; - create, present, and defend a simple marketing plan for a given product; - realize a simple marketing research; - discuss the societal effects of marketing.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 20 %: 20 % Term paper 30 %: 30 % Mid-term test(s) 10 %: 10 % Final test 40 %: 40 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: Armstrong, G., & Kotler, P. (2021). Principles of marketing. Pearson. SHARP, Byron. How brands grow : what marketers don't know. South Melbourne: Oxford University Press, 2010. ISBN 978-0-19-557356-5.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is focused on advanced mathematical concepts in business economics, finance and economic theory. The aim of the course is to make the students familiar with basic terms and methods in the field of linear algebra and infinitesimal calculus and show their practical use in economic applications.
course_content: 1. Functions of one variable and their use in economics. Function transformations and their use in comparative statics. 2. Limit of a function, continuity. 3. Derivatives. Rules for differentiation, L’Hospital’s rule. Derivatives of higher order. 4. Local extrema, convexity, investigation of functions. Applications in economics. 5. Integrals, basic formulas, per partes and substitution methods, definite integrals and their use in economics. 6. Functions of several variables, partial derivatives, graphical interpretation and use in economics. Differential, total differential, elasticity. 6. Multivariate optimization.
learning_outcomes: solve the following tasks: 1) use mathematical methods to solve economic equilibria; 2) determine the limit of a function, the derivative of functions, calculate limits using L'Hospital rule. 3) investitage functions; 4) use functions of several variables and calculate partial derivatives; 5) calculate integrals using per partes and substitution methods. 6) solve systems of linear equations using Gauss elimination and Cramer's rule; 7) solve matrix equations, determine the inverse matrix and calculate determinants;
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 10 %: 10 % Mid-term test(s) 30 %: 30 % Final test 60 %: 60 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: Chiang, A. C., & Wainwright, K. (2005). Fundamental methods of mathematical economics. McGraw-Hill. Klůfa, J., & Kaspříková, N. (2013). Mathematics for economic universities (1st ed.). Ekopress. Recommended: Hoy, M., Livernois, J., & McKenna, C. (1996). Mathematics for economics. Addison-Wesley.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces students to basic economic concepts and relations between them and thus create conditions for the study of other economic disciplines.
course_content: 1. The subject and method of economics. 2. Theory of the Consumer: consumer substitution, budget constraint, cardinal and ordinal approach. 3. Demand: demand curve, substitution and income effects, price elasticity, income and cross-elasticity of demand, consumer surplus. 4. Theory of the firm: transaction costs and the nature of the firm; goals of a firm; the production function and costs: short run, long run; technical substution. 5. Revenues, demand. 6. Equilibrium of a firm on a perfect market. 7. Market equilibrium and market price; benefits of exchange, changes in equilibrium, state interventions. 8. Imperfect markets: price creation on an imperfect market, monopoly, monopolistic competition, oligopoly. 9. Alternative goals of firms, principal-agent problem. 10. Agriculture market. 11. Labour markets: production function and labour demand, market supply of labour, equilibrium of the labour market, trade unions. 12. Capital market: Trade-off between current and future consumption: time preference, optimal combination of current and future consumption. Optimum of firm, capital demand. 13. Equilibrium and efficiency: efficiency in production and allocation efficiency in different market structures. 14. Market failures and state interventions. Externalities, social and private costs and benefits, property rights and the Coase theorem. Public goods and public choice: non-excludability and the free-rider problem, non-rivalry. Asymetric information, Monopoly. 15. Microeconomic state policy.
learning_outcomes: - describe mechanism of economy - apply basic economic terms and models - analyze economic problems from microeconomic subjects - apply principles of consumer behaviour - apply principles of market behaviour - explain the relationship between economic theory and economic reality
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 10 %: 10 % Mid-term test(s) 20 %: 20 % Final test 20 %: 20 % Final oral exam 50 %: 50 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: Mankiw, N. G. (2009). Principles of microeconomics. South-Western/Cengage Learning. Bernanke, B., Frank, R. H., & Johnston, L. (2009). Principles of microeconomics. McGraw-Hill/Irwin. Recommended: NICHOLSON, Walter; SNYDER, Christopher M. Intermediate microeconomics and its application. Mason: South-Western Cengage Learning, 2010. ISBN 978-0-324-59910-7.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course explores the structures of real organisations with the aim of understand the reasons of using these structures and their limitations. During the course the students will compare the structures in the literature with the structures used by organisations. The course aims to provide the basic knowledge about the current structures and their use and the importance of flexibility principle of the structures.
course_content: • Introduction and Organisational Architecture definition • Understanding organisations • Organisational structure Characteristics • Traditional Organisational structures • Organisational structure analysis • Big corporations • Corporate governance • New Trends in organisational structures • Flexibility, agile, lean, start-ups
learning_outcomes: - understand the concept of organisations and their inherent parts. - determine the characteristics of Organisational Structures in order to identify them in real organisations. - recognise different types of traditional Organisational Structures to comprehend the possibilities of structures among organisations. - argue advantages and disadvantages of traditional Organisational Structures to identify best possible fit under specific circumstances. - obtain information about the Organisational Structure from organisation’s annual reports to learn from different organisational sources. - implement an organisational analysis using Star Model and Mintzberg Model as two basic approaches to understand the current situation from organisational theory. - understand the concept of Corporate Governance and its effects on organisations. - identify Corporate Governance models to understand the reasons of these bodies within the organisation. - argue advantages and disadvantages of Corporate Governance models as an enlargement of the knowledge of the concepts. - explain the concept of turbulence and its impact on organisational structures and corporate governance. - recognise different types of new trends for Organisational Structures, their reasons and possibilities. - argue advantages and disadvantages of new trends of Organisational Structures to identify best possible fit under specific circumstances.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 10 %: 10 % Term paper 30 %: 30 % Presentation or defence 10 %: 10 % Final test 50 %: 50 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: Brickley, J. A., Smith, C. W., & Zimmerman, J. L. (2009). Managerial economics and organizational architecture. McGraw-Hill. Recommended: International differences in entrepreneurship [elektronický zdroj]. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2010. Harvard business review on corporate governance. Boston: Harvard Business School, 2000. ISBN 1-57851-237-9. Harvard business review on green business strategy. [Boston]: Harvard Business School Press, 2007. ISBN 978-1-4221-2108-5.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: Organizational behavior course serves as an introduction to psychological and sociological topics applied in organizational environment. The course is focused on individual (personality, attitudes, emotions) and interpersonal (communication, teamwork, power) aspects of organizational life. The course focuses also on theories and models of motivation, leadership, organizational culture and change in a variety of organizational settings.
course_content: 1. Introduction - A Framework of OB. 2. Methodology. 3. Individual Processes: Personality. 4. Individual Processes: Emotions and Attitudes. 5. Individual Processes: Perception and Attributions. 6. Individual Processes: Motivation. 7. Interpersonal Processes: Interpersonal Communication. 8. Interpersonal Processes: Power and Persuasion. 9. Interpersonal Processes: Teamwork. 10. Interpersonal Processes: Leadership. 11. Organizational Processes: Organizational Culture. 12. Organizational Processes: Organizational Change. 13. Course Wrap Up and Final Test.
learning_outcomes: The student should learn essential terms and concepts of psychology and sociology to better understand and explain behavior of an individual and a group in the organizational environment. The course offers orientation and essential experience in methodology used in relevant topics. Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to: - describe and explain key factors that influence individual behavior in organization (personality, group and organizational factors); - assess benefits and limits of various sources of information linked to organizational practice; - catch the complexity of social phenomenon and discuss (potential) weak as well as strong points in relevant topic studies.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 20 %: 20 % Term paper 30 %: 30 % Presentation or defence 15 %: 15 % Mid-term test(s) 15 %: 15 % Final test 20 %: 20 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: McKenna, E. F. (2012). Business psychology and organizational behaviour. Psychology Press. Recommended: Griffin, R. W., Gully, S. M., & Phillips, J. (2017). Organizational behaviour : managing people and organizations. Cengage Learning. Buchanan, D. A., & Huczynski, A. A. (2013). Organizational behaviour. Pearson.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces modern principles and practices of personnel management (human resource management) in the organization, explains the key role of personnel management in achieving strategic objectives of the organization and introduces fundamental human resource functions that determine the competitiveness of the organization in the global economy.
course_content: 1. Organization of personnel management (allowance 4/4) 2. Job design and job analysis (allowance 2/2) 3. Personnel planning (allowance 2/2) 4. Employee recruitment and selection (allowance 2/2) 5. Employee hiring and orientation (allowance 2/2) 6. Performance management and appraisal (allowance 4/4) 7. Employee compensation and benefits (allowance 2/2) 8. Employee training and development (allowance 2/2) 9. Working conditions and labour relations (allowance 4/4) 10. Human resource information system (allowance 2/2)
learning_outcomes: 1. Explain the concept and objectives of personnel management in the organization; describe the system of human resource functions; characterize the role of managers and HR specialists. 2. Explain the concept of a job in the organization; characterize different approaches to job design; carry out the job analysis; create job descriptions. 3. Explain the concept and objectives of personnel planning; define the process of personnel planing; propose solutions of labour shortage or surplus. 4. Explain the concept and objectives of employee recruitment and selection; characterize different methods of recruitment; formulate job advertisement; carry out curriculum vitae analysis and selection interview. 5. Explain the concept and objectives of employee hiring and orientation; define contract of employment; design an orientation program. 6. Explain the concept and objectives of performance management; characterize different methods of performance appraisal; carry out an appraisal interview. 7. Explain the concept and objectives of employee training and development; identify needs and define plan of training and development; characterize different on the job and off the job training and development methods; assess results of training and development. 8. Explain the concept and objectives of employee compensation; characterize different forms of remuneration; describe wage system; characterize employee benefits. 9. Explain the concept and objectives of working conditions and labour relations, particularly organization of working time, creation of working environment, safety and health protection, individual and collective labour relations. 10. Explain the concept and objectives of application of the human resource information system, characterize application of information and communication technologies in personnel management.

Prentice Hall/Pearson Education International. BOXALL, Peter F.; PURCELL, John. Strategy and human resource management. London: Palgrave Macmillan Education, 2016. ISBN 978-1-137-40763-4. Recommended: ARMSTRONG, Michael. Armstrong's handbook of performance management : an evidence-based guide to delivering high performance. London: Kogan Page, 2009. ISBN 978-0-7494-5392-3. ARMSTRONG, Michael. Armstrong's handbook of reward management practice : an evidence-based guide to improving performance through reward. London: Kogan Page, 2015. ISBN 978-0-7494-7389-1. Heery, E., & Noon, M. (2008). A dictionary of human resource management. Oxford University Press. Storey, J. (1995). Human resource management : a critical text. Routledge. NEWELL, Helen; SCARBROUGH, Harry. Human resource management in context : a case study approach. New York: Palgrave, 2002. ISBN 0-333-92135-6.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to familiarize students with the basic approaches of operations management for manufacture and non-manufacturing organisations. Introduce basic methods of production processes management, but also management of support and auxiliary processes. Furthermore, the course also aims to give basic orientation about product portfolio of companies, legal requirements for operational processes and products as well as product quality requirements. Also, the course aims to familiarize students with practical application of selected approaches and methods with focus on modern methods of production and inventory management.
course_content: 1. The nature and objectives of OM, links to strategic (tactical) management and other areas of management. Typology of production processes, technology or product facilities layout. Process flows. Core Business, Facility Management, Outsourcing. 2. Information resources to manage production and services, the importance of standardization, standards at the organisation. Standards of material consumption, available work time, work standards, capacity standards. 3. Standardization of production activities (production batch, continuous time manufacture or process to provide services, beat, rhythm, Cycle-Time). 4. Product portfolio, product lines and groups. Product portfolio diagnosis and strategy. Added value for customers, strategy innovation. Legislation for operations. 5. Pre-production stage of the product. Innovative activity, product design, technology design. 6. Operational management of production. Operational Plan, capacity plan, technological aspects and variants of manufacturing processes. Specifics of operational processes in services. 7. Modern methods of production and inventory management. Principles of Lean, Push-Pull, TPS, TOC. Methods of ABC, JIT, JIS, Kanban. 8. Support processes. Facility management. Ensuring uptime of machinery and equipment, systems maintenance, the TPM. 9. Internal logistics, storage in operational activities, logistics, warehousing, inventory optimization capabilities. Material and information flows, handling equipment and handling units. 10. Quality management in operating activities, measurement of product quality and processes, the principles of Kaizen and Six Sigma. 11. Operational management aims. Operations system boundaries, status and role of operational manager at top management level.
learning_outcomes: After successful completion of the course, students will be able to: - Define the basic concepts and the basic relations of production processes. - Explain the role of the supporting processes for the production processes management. - Describe the basics of modern methods of production and inventory management. - Prove calculate operational standards (capacity, consumption, material, management). - Explain the basic relations of warehousing capacity. - Characterize quality management. - Characterize the importance of pre-stages (product design, process design) in relation with product management. - Apply the method of technological variants choice. - Plan the schedule time structure of production processes. - To practice on production capacity problems and overall equipment effectiveness calculations (OEE).
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 10 %: 10 % Presentation or defence 10 %: 10 % Mid-term test(s) 30 %: 30 % Final test 50 %: 50 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: Brandon-Jones, A., & Slack, N. (2019). Operations management. Pearson. COX, Jeff; GOLDRATT, Eliyahu M. The goal : a process of ongoing improvement. Great Barrington: North River Press, 2004. ISBN 0-88427-178-1.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is designed to develop students' understanding of consumers and their behaviour, which is a key prerequisite to the development of an adequate marketing programme.
course_content: The course introduces the main determinants of consumer behaviour, covering the buying decision process and its key psychological, social, and cultural influences. Students will complete two group projects applying course concepts in practice. In the first, groups select a brand and analyse it throughout the semester, culminating in an original advertisement that demonstrates a chosen consumer behaviour concept. In the second, students carry out a consumer behaviour experiment and present their findings in a short video. The students will be required to participate actively in the lectures, discussing their results. The course is focused in particular on the following areas: - Definition of consumer behaviour it today's world and its importance for marketing - Buying behaviour and consumer decision making Psychological factors in consumer behaviour: - Perception of the outside world, perception of the self - Motivation, values - Learning, memory Social factors in consumer behaviour: - Reference groups and consumer behaviour - Economic factors and consumer behaviour - Culture and consumer behaviour - Consumer culture, transformations in consumer culture, their importance for marketing and the role of marketing in consumer culture
learning_outcomes: - Explain the central role of the consumer in the market offerings of companies and other organizations; - Apply theories and models of consumer behavior within the formulation of a company's marketing strategies; - Identify what information about consumers needs to be gathered for marketing purposes; - Characterize the different types of values offered by companies to the end customer and to society as a whole.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation 10 %: 10 % Group performance 30 %: 30 % Final test 60 %: 60 % Total 100 %: 100 %
literature: Basic: Askegaard, S., Bamossy, G. J., Hogg, M. K., & Solomon, M. R. (2019). Consumer behaviour : a European perspective. Pearson. Recommended: Solomon, M. R. (2010). Consumer behaviour : a European perspective Michael Solomon ... [at al.]. Prentice Hall. Antonides, G., & Raaij, W. F. v. (1998). Consumer behaviour : a European perspective. John Wiley & Sons. Consumer behavior and culture : consequences for global marketing and advertising. Sage. ARNOULD, E.J., PRICE, L., ZINKHAN, G.M.,Consumers, McGraw-Hill/Irwin, 2 ed. 2004, ISBN 0-07-253714-0 AVERY, J. , KOZINETS, R., , BANWARI M., RAGHUBIR,P., WOODSIDE, A.: Consumer Behaviour, International 3rd ed., Open Mentis, Cincinnati 2013, ISBN 978-0-979-1336-4-0

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the first part of this course is to get students acquainted with descriptive approaches to statistical analysis: basic descriptive statistics, descriptive analysis of dependence and descriptive time series analysis. The aim of the second part is to familiarize students with elements of probability as well as the basics of deductive and inductive reasoning. Students will also learn the principles of the most commonly used statistical methods and the conditions of their use. The general aim of the course is to teach students to correctly interpret the results and understand and use the presentation options of the outcomes.
course_content: Elementary descriptive statistics (frequencies, measures of central tendency and variation, statistical graphs/charts) Descriptive contingency table analysis, descriptive analysis of variance Descriptive correlation analysis and descriptive regression analysis Descriptive time series analysis (elementary descriptives, time series decomposition, smoothing, ...) Basics of probability theory Random variables and their probability distributions The most important probability distributions Essential of sampling and statistical inference (estimates, hypotheses testing, selected tests) Index numbers (types of indices and differences, simple indices, aggregate indices, ...)
learning_outcomes: apply the most important statistical methods. Further, students will be able to select suitable statistical method for the description and analysis of a given problem and interpret the results obtained with the use of the statistical method applied. More precisely, students will be able to recognize types of variables in a data set, perform basic descriptive statistical analysis using frequency distribution tables and measures of central tendency, perform basic descriptive statistical analysis using measures of variation, understand basic concepts of probability theory and calculate basic examples on probability. Further, they will understand theoretical concepts of random variables and will be competent to study basic properties of probability distributions. They will recognize practical situations for employing selected probability distributions and perform basic calculations with the most important and common probability distributions. Moreover, students will be able to distinguish between population survey and sample survey (data analysis), calculate basic point estimates, calculate and interpret selected basic confidence intervals, students will understand basic principles (and terminology) of statistical hypotheses testing and will be able to perform several basic statistical hypotheses tests and interpret their results. Students will be acquainted with studying relationship of two qualitative variables using contingency coefficients and a statistical hypothesis test as well as relationship of one qualitative and one quantitative variable using effect size and a statistical hypothesis test. Students will understand basic principles (and terminology) of linear regression analysis, they will be able to study relationship of two quantitative variables using regression line, fit the regression line for a data set and judge the quality of fitted regression line using coefficient of determination. Additionally, students will understand basic principles of partial t-test of the slope, will be able to compare different fitted regression functions using adjusted coefficient of determination and study linear relationship of two quantitative variables using correlation coefficient and a statistical hypothesis test. Students will have knowledge allowing them to distinguish different types of time series, perform basic descriptive analysis of time series, understand basic principles of time series decomposition and express trend component of a time series using basic trend functions. Students will be also able to express trend component of a time series using moving averages, perform basic forecasting of a time series using trend functions, disgtinguish different forms of a seasonal component and express seasonal component by simple approach and by regression approach with dummy variables. Finally, students will be able to distinguish different types of indices (simple, composite, aggregate), calculate selected composite and aggregate indices and will understand basic principles of inflation rate calculation.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 40 % Final test: 60 %
literature: Basic: Weiss, N. A. (2012). Elementary statistics. Addison-Wesley. Trešl, J. (1998). Success in statistics. Vysoká škola ekonomická. Recommended: Benson, P. G., McClave, J. T., & Sincich, T. (2018). Statistics : for business and economics. Pearson.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The main purpose of this course is to help students gather a deep insight into the methods of a comprehensive and complex strategic analysis useful for any further strategic decisions within the company. Students will be provided with a “strategist´s toolbox” of most common frameworks used to understand both internal and external environment of the company.
course_content: 1. Introduction to strategic analysis 2. Problem solving 3. Understanding world megatrends 4. Strategizing - setting corporate goals, missioning, visioning 5. Stakeholder analysis 6. External environment analysis 7. Competitive analysis 8. Internal resources and capabilities analysis 9. Performance analysis 10. Scenario planning 11. Competitive strategies I 12. Competitive strategies II Detailed course content and learning outcomes of each lecture will be sent to students at the beginning of the semester.
learning_outcomes: - distinguish the level of relevancy and usefulness of available data and information required for quality strategic analysis, - identify and recognize current and possible future megatrends with a potential impact on the company, - define major corporate goals and formulate corporate mission and vision statements, - describe different stakeholder groups, identify their expectations and potential conflicts, - assess macroeconomics factors with a future impact on the company and analyse key players and their power in the industry, - critically evaluate real sources of competitive advantages of the company, - construct different future scenarios of company evolution reflecting external and internal environment of the company, - use a wide palette of tools and frameworks to analyse corporate strategic position, - recognize different competitive strategies, critically evaluate their suitability for given company and eventually suggest adjustments of pursued strategies.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces students to the main legal systems, to the sources of law with respect to national and international laws as well as the law of the European Union. The course surveys the functioning of the EU institutions and EU Law, including competition law. Also, it deals with practical aspects related to starting a business, such as the setting up business corporations, the law of contracts, labor law and business ethics and compliance.
course_content: 1.Concept of Law, Sources of International, EU and National Law, Legal Systems (allowance 2/0) 2.Doing Business in the Czech Republic (allowance 2/0) 3.International Public Law (allowance 2/0) 4.EU Institutions (allowance 2/0) 5.EU Law (allowance 2/0) 6.Czech Constitutional Law and the Protection of Human Rights in the Czech Rep. and the EU (allowance 2/0) 7.Labor Law (allowance 2/0) 8.Legal liability (allowance 2/0) 9.Competition Law (allowance 2/0) 10.Introduction to the Law of Contracts (allowance 2/0) 11.Introduction to Corporate Law (allowance 2/0) 12.Procedural law - Litigation and ADR (allowance 2/0) 13.Business Ethics and Corporate Social Responsibility (allowance 2/0)
learning_outcomes: - decide which legal norm will be applied in a particular legal context (national law vs. EU law) - set up their own business as a self-employed person - explain the essence of contract law and corporate law - identify the rules of business ethics and compliance with respect to the selected form of doing business
