course_title: Applied deep learning and artificial intelligence
university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course provides students with a well-rounded understanding of deep learning and artificial intelligence that integrates technical, business and human perspectives. By balancing these perspectives, students will be better equipped to apply deep learning to real-world business problems and make informed decisions about designing and developing artificial intelligence solutions.
course_content: - Deep learning and artificial intelligence concepts, tasks, and architectures - Deep learning use cases - Preparation and handling of various data types (image, speech, written language, spatial and sensor data) - Training models using deep learning in Python - Performance evaluation and optimization of deep learning - Business perspective of artificial intelligence solutions - Intelligent automation - Human-centered approaches in artificial intelligence solutions development
learning_outcomes: - clarify the role of deep learning in use cases, such as fraud detection, wear detection, quality control, demand prediction, content recommendation, and choose related deep learning tasks, such as object detection from image and video, image segmentation, emotion recognition, speech to text conversion, text or sound classification - choose appropriate deep learning architectures for use cases - interpret deep learning metrics and design a learning process - design a solution based on artificial intelligence, taking into account the technical, business and human perspective - train and evaluate a model using deep learning - explain the role of human-centered approaches in the development of artificial intelligence - assess the benefits and costs of solutions built on artificial intelligence - evaluate compliance with the rules and ethical principles of solutions built on artificial intelligence - evaluate compliance with the regulations and ethical principles of solutions built on artificial intelligence - describe the principle and list examples of intelligent automation
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Term paper: 40 % Presentation or defence: 10 % Mid-term test(s): 30 %
literature: Basic: GÉRON, Aurélien. Hands-on machine learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras and TensorFlow : concepts, tools, and techniques to build intelligent systems. Beijing: O'Reilly, 2019. ISBN 978-1-4920-3264-9. Recommended: KAMATH, Uday; LIU, John; WHITAKER, James. Deep learning for NLP and speech recognition. Cham: Springer, 2019. ISBN 978-3-030-14598-9.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The subject focuses on the specification and explanation of the basic building blocks in the field of business analytics (BA) architecture, both theoretically and practically. The purpose is to clarify the different types of architectures in the enterprise as a whole and in BA, their principles and characteristics and the approaches derived from them through creation. Furthermore, understand the meaning and relationship between individual layers and types of architectures, understand data, data sources, data structures and the possibilities of accessing and using data.
course_content: Before starting the course, it is mandatory to complete the following study materials in the scope - it will be checked (56 hours, which is 75x45 minutes of teaching):: - DataCamp (total 12h) • Data Warehousing Concepts - DataCamp Learn • Understanding Modern Data Architecture - DataCamp Learn • Data Strategy - DataCamp Learn • Data Management Concepts - DataCamp Learn - materials in Moodle (total 16h) • prezentace • zdroje k DataVault - external information sources (total 10h): • Project A Data Modelling Best Practices Part I: How to Model Data in a Data Warehouse? • Project A Data Modelling Best Practices Part II: How to Build a Data Warehouse? - YouTube • What Exactly is a Data Warehouse? • Deciphering Data Architectures (Modern DW, Data Fabric, Data Lakehouse, Data Mesh) - James Serra - … - Základy DAMA v knize DAMA-DMBOK, viz povinná literatura (celkem 16h) Recommended to complete (2h): - Data Warehousing Trends, Best Practices, and Future Outlook - James Serra - BI Conference - Jun 2022 - Data Lakehouse, Data Mesh, and Data Fabric - James Serra - DataMinutes - Jan 2022 - YouTube - Choosing the Right Data Warehouse Modelling Approach by Andy Sawyer Data Training Lab - Inmon vs Kimball: Data Warehousing Approaches In the course: Block 1 - Introduction to the course - Introduction to the issue of architectures and what architectures are - Basic introduction to individual architectures and their mutual relationships and influence on other layers of architectures - Architectural standards - DAMA, ISO - Certification in the field of modeling and design of architectures (enterprise, data-oriented, etc.) Block 2 - Data and data benefits Block 3 - Architecture of key supporting areas (Data quality, MDM, RDM, Metadata Management) Block 4 - Operation of data-oriented solutions and assessment of their maturity Block 5 - Continuous case study Continuous work in teams on team tasks. Attendance at least 80%. A prerequisite for studying the course is to complete the defined pre-read, which will be tested. If you do not meet the minimum level of knowledge from the pre-read, you will not be able to complete the course.
learning_outcomes: discuss: - The basics of the work of an enterprise, application, infrastructure, data, etc. architect - Benefits and the importance of enterprise, application, data architecture for the organization - Individual types of architecture, principles and characteristics - Procedures for the design of the basic building blocks of architecture in the field of business analytics - What aspects must be addressed in connection with building and managing the architecture of business analytics in the company - How a clearly and correctly defined architecture contributes to the sustainability of the development of business analytics in the organization - Design the enterprise architecture, design and model the architecture of a data-oriented solution in detail according to the requirements of a specific company
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 10 % Problem based learning assessment: 30 % Final oral exam: 60 %
literature: Basic: SLÁNSKÝ, David. Data a analytika pro 21. století. [Průhonice]: Professional Publishing, 2018. ISBN 978-80-88260-25-7. BRACKETT, Michael H.; EARLEY, Susan; MOSLEY, Mark. The DAMA guide to the data management body of knowledge : (DAMA-DMBOK guide). Bradley Beach: Technics Publications, 2010. ISBN 978-1-935504-02-3. EARLEY, Susan. DAMA-DMBOK : data management body of knowledge. Basking Ridge: Technics Publications, 2017. ISBN 978-1-63462-234-9. Recommended: ASPLEN-TAYLOR, Simon. Data and analytics strategy for business : unlock data assets and increase innovation with a results-driven data strategy. London: Kogan Page, 2022. ISBN 978-1-3986-0607-4. ASHDOWN, Jessi; ERYUREK, Evren; GILAD, Uri; KIBUNGUCHY-GRANT, Anita; LAKSHMANAN, Valliappa. Data governance : the definitive guide : people, processes, and tools to operationalize data trustworthiness. Beijing: O'Reilly, 2021. ISBN 978-1-492-06349-0. INMON, William H.; LEVINS, Mary; LINSTEDT, Daniel. Data architecture : a primer for the data scientist. London: Academic Press, An imprint of Elsevier, 2019. ISBN 978-0-12-816916-2. STRENGHOLT, Piethein. Data management at scale : modern data architecture with data mesh and data fabric. Santa Rosa: O'Reilly, 2023. ISBN 978-1-098-13886-8. TUPPER, Charles D. Data architecture : from zen to reality. Burlington: Morgan Kaufmann, 2011. ISBN 978-0-12-385126-0.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course provides an in-depth overview of Competitive Intelligence (CI) in the context of web environments and its importance in modern business operations. Through a combination of theoretical concepts, practical exercises, and case studies, students will learn how to gather and analyze information about their competitors and the competitive landscape in which they operate, utilizing both surface web and deep web sources.
course_content: - Definition and scope of CI - History of CI and its role in modern business operations - Legal and ethical considerations in CI - Web environments as sources of data, information, and knowledge - Advanced search methods for both surface web and deep web sources - Tools and techniques for data collection and analysis - Understanding the Competitive Intelligence lifecycle - Identifying Key Intelligence Topics (KITs) and relevant sources - Setting up information requirements and collecting data - Analyzing and interpreting data to identify patterns and trends - Developing actionable insights and recommendations - Predicting future developments in the competitive landscape - Understanding types of IP databases and their use in CI - Analyzing competitors' IP strategy - Managing and protecting intellectual property - Effective communication strategies for CI findings - Preparing and presenting reports and presentations to key stakeholders - Developing visualizations and dashboards to communicate insights - Applications of CI in product development, marketing, and other business operations - Case studies of successful CI implementation - Future trends in CI and its role in the evolving business landscape - Artificial Intelligence and CI
learning_outcomes: - To understand web environments as sources of data, information, and knowledge. - To be able to use advanced search methods for both surface web and deep web sources. - To understand the Competitive Intelligence lifecycle and implement it into the company's structure. - To be able to identify Key Intelligence Topics (KIT) and following Key Intelligence Questions (KIQ), set up information requirements and determine the relevant sources. - Students will discuss the problem of weak signals, how to capture them, and transform them into invaluable insights that could shape business strategy. - Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of intellectual property management, types of IP databases, and how to understand the IP strategy of their competition. - To prepare external data and information found for further analysis.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Term paper: 30 % Presentation or defence: 30 % Final test: 20 %
literature: Basic: MOLNÁR, Zdeněk. Competitive intelligence, aneb, Jak získat konkurenční výhodu. Praha: Oeconomica, 2012. ISBN 978-80-245-1908-1. Recommended: De las Heras-Rosas C, Herrera J. Innovation and competitive intelligence in business. A bibliometric analysis. International Journal of Financial Studies. 2021 Jun 10;9(2):31. Hristovski R, Balagué N. Theory of cooperative-competitive intelligence: principles, research directions, and applications. Frontiers in Psychology. 2020 Sep 11;11:2220. Ranjan J, Foropon C. Big data analytics in building the competitive intelligence of organizations. International Journal of Information Management. 2021 Feb 1;56:102231. Wu Q, Yan D, Umair M. Assessing the role of competitive intelligence and practices of dynamic capabilities in business accommodation of SMEs. Economic Analysis and Policy. 2023 Mar 1;77:1103-14.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course focuses on the explanation and practical application of basic principles, use cases and issues related to Business Intelligence in the scope of data warehouses and reporting. The purpose is both to explain the necessary theory and architectural concepts and to provide students with an environment in which to try out a practical solution to a business problem from analysis to the implementation of the required reports over a data warehouse integrating data from several source systems.
course_content: Introduction - Basic principles and concepts - Introduction to reporting, principles of report design - Introduction to data warehouse modeling - Typical problems (Inconsistent results from different sources; Inconsistent understanding of reported results; Data (in)quality) - Position within the architecture (DWH; ETL; Reporting / Dashboarding; Self-service BI; Data Lake; OLAP / OLTP) - Task (Presentation of the company; Presentation of the solved problem; Presentation of the IS/ICT environment) - Tasks (Identification of business requirements; Identification of necessary metrics and dimensions; Design of reports according to identified requirements; Creation of functional specifications of reports) Architecture - Architectures - different approaches to building data warehouses (Kimball / Inmon, Data Vault - agile DWH development), reporting - EDWH (Landing; Stage - role, modeling; Core - role, modeling; marty) - ETL / ELT - Task (Design high-level architecture of the target solution) - Tasks (Identification of the necessary data in the source systems; Design and implementation of the DWH stage and core) Data pumps - Possible ways to implement ETL / ELT - Traffic control needs related to data pumps - Integration Services - Task (Design and implementation of data warehouse) - Tasks (Implementation of pumps for the stage; Implementation of pumps for the core; Design and implementation of the DWH stage and core) OLAP, Tabular model, SSBI - OLAP, Tabular model - principles and brief introduction - Analysis Services - Self-service reporting tools - a brief introduction - Task (Design of data analysis layer as preparation for reporting) - Tasks (Completing the implementation of pumps for the core; Implementing the analysis layer) Reporting - Reporting - best practices - Reporting tool - introduction, introduction to creating reports, dashboarding - Task (Draft reports) - Tasks (Implementation of reports, dashboarding; Preparation of a presentation with a solution to the presented real business problem) Conclusion - Presentation of outputs - Course summary
learning_outcomes: understand and discuss: - Purpose and benefit of reporting - Possible problems arising from the use of data for analytical purposes - Data warehouse architectures - Procedures for design and implementation of Business Intelligence solutions - data warehouse and reporting Students will be able to independently implement a reporting solution corresponding to the problems identified in the given organization.
assessment_methods: Term paper: 60 % Presentation or defence: 10 % Final oral exam: 30 %
literature: Basic: SLÁNSKÝ, David. Data a analytika pro 21. století. [Průhonice]: Professional Publishing, 2018. ISBN 978-80-88260-25-7. KNAFLIC, Cole Nussbaumer. Storytelling with data : a data visualization guide for business professionals. Hoboken: Wiley, 2015. ISBN 978-1-119-00225-3. Recommended: MARYŠKA, Miloš; NOVOTNÝ, Ota; POUR, Jan. Business intelligence v podnikové praxi. Praha: Professional Publishing, 2012. ISBN 978-80-7431-065-2.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course provides an essential understanding of the principles behind the emerging and evolving field of Machine Learning Operations (MLOps). Students will be equipped with fundamental concepts of DevOps, Data Science and Machine Learning in the MLOps realm. This course will cover the entire model lifecycle using Open Source technologies and offering a glimpse to commercial tools as well.
course_content: Introduction to MLOps - Overview of DevOps and MLOps - Challenges of deploying and maintaining machine learning models at scale - MLops lifecycle MLOps Infrastructure - Version Control (Git and GitLab) - Cloud native technologies (Docker, Kubernetes) - Containerization - Orchestration - Feature Store and Model Registry - Automation Platforms - MLOps design patterns Data Acquisition and Preprocessing - Data Collection, hashing and Storage technologies - Data Cleaning and Preprocessing - Version Control Systems for Data and code Model Experimentation - Experimentation trackers - Automated Model Training and Validation - Model candidates and version control ML Systems deployment - Deployment patterns - Deployment to Cloud Platforms - Model Monitoring and Maintenance - Model Drift and re-training Evaluation Methods - Hands-on assignment
learning_outcomes: Student will -Understand the concepts of DevSecOps, Data Science and Machine Learning and how they are integrated in MLOps. - Understand the challenges of deploying and maintaining machine learning models at scale. - Learn open source technologies used in MLOps, including version control, continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD), containerization, cloud-native deployments, DevSecOps Platforms. - Build MLops pipelines, including project structure, code review, data preprocessing, model development, model testing, deployment and monitoring. - Learn Software Engineering practices applied into Data Science
assessment_methods: Term paper: 30 % Mid-term test(s): 40 % Final oral exam: 30 %
literature: Recommended: Yaron Haviv, Noah Gift, Implementing MLOps in the Enterprise, O'Reilly Media, Inc., 2023, ISBN: 9781098136581 Galindez W., Learn DevOps and MLOps Workloads with GitLab, Packt, planned for 2023

course_title: Advanced machine learning and big data
university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course focuses on expanding knowledge and skills in the field of machine learning beyond the bachelor's degree and further on big data processing. Students will work with modern machine learning techniques and algorithms for analyzing large data sets. They will also deal with work with distributed computing and cloud services.
course_content: - Repetition and extension of machine learning tasks discussed in basic undergraduate courses - From correlation to causation, a practical toolkit for causal questions - Sharper decisions under uncertainty - Selected special tasks, e.g. propensity tasks or ranking models. - Application of machine learning to time series, sequential and temporal data. - Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) - Machine learning in survival analysis - Frameworks for big data processing - Data processing: batch vs stream, MapReduce - Data analysis: visualization, modeling with PySpark - Tools for Big data, ecosystems and platforms
learning_outcomes: be able to: - Understand and apply advanced machine learning techniques. - Connect a practical assignment with the information contained in the data and use machine learning procedures to solve it. - Solve applied tasks beyond the basic machine learning procedures discussed in the bachelor's degree. - Analyze and process large data sets and use modern machine learning algorithms for this.
assessment_methods: Term paper: 30 % Mid-term test(s): 40 % Final oral exam: 30 %
literature: Basic: GÉRON, Aurélien. Hands-on machine learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras and TensorFlow : concepts, tools, and techniques to build intelligent systems. Beijing: O'Reilly, 2019. ISBN 978-1-4920-3264-9.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course provides students with practical experience on specific projects. On these projects, students apply the knowledge gained in previous courses. Students will be involved in projects and assignments in a selected company or public institution during the course. The course thus represents a suitable bridge between theoretical studies and the student's future profession. The internship is carried out in cooperation with partner companies or public institutions on the basis of a partnership agreement.
course_content: - Before enrolment, the student contacts the teacher who is listed in the internship offer and who acts as a tutor. The student will receive a framework contract to define the fundamental rights and obligations of the parties (FIS, student, recipient organization). After signing it by the parties, the student is enrolled to this course. - After enrolment, the aims of the internship are set with the tutor and supervisor of the practice of the receiving organization. - The internship is supervised by a supervisor and tutor. - Finally, the student will prepare a report on the progress and results of the internship, which includes the student's assessment of the practice of the receiving organization. - Grading is awarded by tutor based on internship assessment, a written report and a practice debate.
learning_outcomes: - Apply knowledge and skills gained in the course of study in performing work tasks - Assess the functioning of the company's or institution's analytics - Present the results of a solved work task
assessment_methods: Practice performance (90 %), Reporting and practice discussion (10 %): 100 %
literature: Basic: Myers, M. D. (2020). Qualitative research in business & management. SAGE. Bothe, O., Kubera, O., Bednář, D., Potančok, M., & Novotný, O. (2022). Data analytics initiatives managing analytics for success. CRC Press Taylor & Francis Group. https://www.successinanalytics.com/

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course develops student’s personal and social skills, mainly: - Problem understanding. - Storytelling. - Creating presentations with impact. - Presentation skills.
course_content: - key output preparation, storyboarding, and structured communication. - creating powerful presentations and practicing presentation skills. - final presentations.
learning_outcomes: - Learn how to create and support a strong story with arguments and information. - Be able to create a powerful presentation. - Gain excellent presentation skills at the top management level. - Be able to work efficiently in a team.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 10 % Presentation or defence: 90 %
literature: Basic: NOVÝ, Ivan; JAROŠOVÁ, Eva; BEDRNOVÁ, Eva et al. (ed.). Manažerská psychologie a sociologie. 1st. vyd. Praha: Management Press, 2012. 615 p. ISBN 978-80-7261-239-0. JAROŠOVÁ, Eva. Trénink sociálních a manažerských dovedností : metodický průvodce. Praha: Management Press, 2005. ISBN 80-7261-135-6.

course_title: Management of Data and Analytics for Business
university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course focuses on managing data and analytics that is strictly aligned with business needs. It covers methods of alignment with business goals, management of services aimed at data utilization, management of projects in data and analytics field and management of financial and organizational aspects of data and analytics applications. Finally, Data Governance and Data Management are discussed as necessary requirements of the sustainable practice of using data to support business in an organization.
course_content: Strategy and business-IT alignment - Strategy of data and analytics - contents and relationships to other components of an organization Services management - Design and implementation of data and analytics services - Evaluation of quality of services - Sourcing (sourcing strategy, tenders, RFPs) - Operations of the business analytics environment - Technological resources - analysis, evaluation, configuration Organizational aspects - Management of human resources for business analytics - Management of relationships with external partners - Organizational aspects impacting Agile architectures and Cloud / hybrid environment applicability Data Governance & Data Management - Data Governance principles, areas of Data Management - Data Management goals definition concerning organizational goals and strategy - Roles in Data Management, data ownership, identification of business requirements on data and their quality - Metadata management Principles and specifics of management of business analytics projects - Project phases and specifics of data-related projects Financial aspects - Management of business analytics economy - Cost calculations, specifics of economic and non-economic effects resulting from business analytics application - Infonomics and Data monetization
learning_outcomes: understand and discuss: - Necessities of management of data and analytics alignment with goals of an organization and how to achieve it - Approaches to managing data-oriented services - Which organizational aspects are crucial concerning data management, what are the necessary roles and responsibilities that need to be established in an organization and for what purposes - How metadata supports the sustainable development of data environment in an organization - What are the specifics and options in the management of data-oriented projects
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 10 % Term paper: 30 % Presentation or defence: 10 % Final oral exam: 50 %
literature: Basic: Potančok, M., Pour, J., & Chramostová, V. (2020). Business analytika v praxi (1st ed.). Oeconomica. https://oeconomica.vse.cz/publikace/business-analytika-v-praxi/ Bothe, O., Kubera, O., Bednář, D., Potančok, M., & Novotný, O. (2022). Data analytics initiatives managing analytics for success. CRC Press Taylor & Francis Group. https://www.successinanalytics.com/ EARLEY, Susan. DAMA-DMBOK : data management body of knowledge. Basking Ridge: Technics Publications, 2017. ISBN 978-1-63462-234-9. Recommended: VOŘÍŠEK, Jiří. Principy a modely řízení podnikové informatiky. Praha: Oeconomica, 2008. ISBN 978-80-245-1440-6.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is designed to show and master the principles of the econometric model building with emphasis on results' economic interpretation and verification. The course presupposes knowledge of statistics and economics at bachelor level.
course_content: 1. Recap of basic concepts, Gauss-Markov Theorem, structure of empirical research 2. Heteroskedasticity and heteroskedasticity robust standard errors 3. Instrumental variables and simultaneous equations 4. Logistic regression (logit) and probit 5. Panel data: Introduction and structure of the panel 6. Panel data: panel data models 7. Introduction to time series 8. Deterministic and stochastic trend, random walk, white noise 9. ARIMA models 10. Stationarity, unit root tests 11. Vector Autoregression (VAR) and its use 12. Error Correction Models (ECM) and their use, cointegration 13. Vector Error Correction Models (VECM)
learning_outcomes: build a simple economic model, find appropriate data and use standard econometric tools to find an answer to research question.
assessment_methods: Term paper: 40 % Final test: 60 %
literature: Basic: Wooldridge, J. M. (2020). Introductory econometrics : a modern approach. Cengage. Recommended: PICKUP, Mark Alexander. Introduction to Time Series Analysis, 2014, ISBN 9781452282015

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course provides students with an understanding of microeconomic, macroeconomic and political aspects of banking, nonbank financial institutions, and financial markets.
course_content: 1. Why study money, banking and financial markets? 2. Nonbank finance 3. The CAPM model 4. Bonds and the behavior of interest rates 5. Commercial banking I 6. Commercial banking II 7. Mid-term test 8. Risk management in banking 9. Regulation of financial markets 10.Central banking 11.Money and understanding interest rates 12.Global financial crises
learning_outcomes: - apply their theoretical knowledge on real-life problems related to both banks and nonbank financial institutions; - explain the main features of financial institutions including commercial banks, central banks, mutual funds, pension funds, insurance companies, private equity, hedge funds and credit rating agencies; - assess the role of regulators and their impact on financial markets.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 70 %
literature: Basic: Mishkin, F. S. (2013). The economics of money, banking, and financial markets. Pearson. MEJSTŘÍK, Michal; PEČENÁ, Magda; TEPLÝ, Petr. Bankovnictví v teorii a praxi = Banking in theory and practice. Praha: Karolinum, 2014. ISBN 978-80-246-2870-7.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The purpose is to teach students to think economically, to familiarize students with basic knowledge of economic science on which they will build the subsequent study of macroeconomics, microeconomics, economic policy and other applied economic disciplines.
course_content: Domestic product and its measurement. Nominal product, real product and price index. Aggregate expenditures. Consumption function. Investment function. Expenditure multiplier. Tax multiplier. Money and money demand. Transaction and precautionary balances. Money creation and money multiplier. Money stock. Money market and its equilibrium. Growth in money stock and its consequences in the short and long runs. Aggregate demand. Aggregate supply. Potential product. Aggregate demand and aggregate supply shocks. Business cycles: Causes, consequences and typology. Economic growth. Sources of growth. Social system and economic growth. Inflation and its measurement. Inflation’s impact on an economy. Cost-push and demand-pull inflation. Adaptive and rational expectations. Phillips curve. Natural rate of unemployment. Exchange rates. Interest rate parity. Purchasing power parity theory. Exchange rate regimes. Money crises and their causes. Balance of payments. Public finances. Tax policy. Theory of bureaucracy. Grey economy and the Laffer curve. Budget deficits and public debt. Structural and cyclical deficit. Crowding-out effect. Political cycle. Monetary policy-goals and instruments. Activist monetary policy. Rational expectation and monetary policy ineffectiveness. Monetary policy regimes: fixed exchange rate, monetary targeting, inflation targeting. Disinflation policy and its consequences in the short and long runs.
learning_outcomes: - analyze theoretically economic issues - describe the relationship between economic theory and economic reality - apply basic principles of human economic behaviour and market functioning on contemporary economic reality and to come up with solutions to applied topics - explain basic economic terms and models in light of most recent scientific evidence, all in the context of the process of globalization - explain, solve and apply models of consumption and investment functions, money market, exchange rates, economic growth and business cycles, unemployment, inflation, taxation and public debt
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 30 % Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 40 %
literature: Basic: Mankiw, N. G. (2012). Principles of economics. South-Western/Cengage Learning. Recommended: Mankiw, N. G. (1998). Principles of macroeconomics. Dryden. Hakes, D. R. (2012). Study guide [for] Mankiw: Principles of economics, sixth edition. South-Western/Cengage Learning. Gordon, R. J. (2000). Macroeconomics. Addison-Wesley. Holman, R. (2011). Ekonomie. C.H. Beck.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The purpose is to teach students to think economically, to familiarize students with basic knowledge of economic science on which they will build the subsequent study of macroeconomics, microeconomics, economic policy and other applied economic disciplines.
course_content: Rational economic decision-making. Consumer choice: indifference analysis. Indifference curves and their behaviour. Budget constraint. Consumer optimum (equilibrium). Demand. Individual demand curves. Substitution and income effects. Price elasticity of demand. Market demand curve. Engel curve. Income elasticity of demand. Inferior and normal goods. Producer choice. Fixed and variable costs. Total and average cost curves. Marginal cost curve. Profit maximization condition. Firm optimum (equilibrium) in the short and long runs. Firm’s supply curves in the short and long runs. Market supply. Price elasticity of supply. Market equilibrium. Reaching the equilibrium. Efficiency of equilibrium. Changes in market equilibrium. Perfectly competitive market. Imperfectly competitive market. Marginal revenue and price-setting under imperfect competition. Firm optimum under imperfect competition. Monopolistic competition. Oligopoly. Imperfect competition and the role of information. Entrepreneurs, information and innovation. Price discrimination. Competition and market entry regulation. Competition and antitrust policies. Asymmetric information. Consumer protection. Competition and monopoly. Monopoly price regulation. State-created monopoly. Natural monopolies. Price agreements (cartels). State price interventions. Price ceiling. Excise tax. Subsidies. State intervention purchases. Production quotas. Production function. Marginal factor product and demand for factors. Optimal choice of technology. Technological substitution. Transfer payments and factor rents. Compensating differentials in wages. Labour markets. Work and leisure trade-off: indifference analysis. Labour supply. Labour market equilibrium. Unemployment. Types of unemployment. Voluntary and involuntary unemployment. Job creation policies. Capital and investment. Time preference and interest rate. Nominal and real interest rate. Loanable funds market: supply, demand, equilibrium. Human capital. Investment decision. Perpetuity and annuity. Rate of yield and risk. Moral hazard. Private and public ownership. Principal-agent problem. Joint-stock companies. Externalities. Property rights. Transaction costs. Coase theorem. Transferable pollution rights. Free (common) resources. Free goods. Public goods. Public choice. Voters’ rational ignorance. Interest groups.
learning_outcomes: - analyze theoretically economic issues - describe the relationship between economic theory and economic reality - apply basic principles of human economic behaviour and market functioning on contemporary economic reality and to come up with solutions to applied topics - explain basic economic terms and models in light of most recent scientific evidence, all in the context of the process of globalization - explain, solve and apply the principles of decision-making of economic agents, market functioning, the nature of competition and regulation, consequences of price regulation, capital and labor markets, externalities and public goods
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 30 % Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 40 %
literature: Basic: Mankiw, N. G. (2012). Principles of economics. South-Western/Cengage Learning. Recommended: Hakes, D. R. (2012). Study guide [for] Mankiw: Principles of economics, sixth edition. South-Western/Cengage Learning. Holman, R. (2011). Ekonomie. C.H. Beck. Mankiw, N. G. (2009). Principles of microeconomics. South-Western/Cengage Learning. Bernanke, B., Frank, R. H., & Johnston, L. (2009). Principles of microeconomics. McGraw-Hill/Irwin.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is a seminar in economic reasoning, developing the practical capability of the economic way of thinking. It is based on reading of economic texts and papers, their synopsizing and summarizing and facilitated discussion of their contents.
course_content: The course is based predominantly on reading of various texts and their subsequent discussion. The topics are chosen by respective instructors. 1) Each week, students are given a text (or texts) related to a chosen topic, which they read. They are then expected to submit a written synopsis of the text(s) of 1800-3600 characters, at least 2 day prior to the class meeting. 2) For each class, students prepare a short presentation (not exceeding 20 min.), consisting of summarizing, extending and/or criticizing the ideas contained in the text(s). For the latter purposes, students are supposed to use additional texts that reply, or are in any other way related, to the original text(s). Generally, students are assumed to use the JSTOR database as a source of the related texts. Students are expected to submit a written synopsis of the presentation of 1800-3600 characters, at least 2 day prior to the class meeting. 3) At the beginning of every class, one student will be randomly picked to present the summary and extensions of the topic, and one student to focus on extensions of the topic only. 4) Students are not required to attend classes. For each missed class (including classes arrived at after the ballot), however, the students must submit their presentation in the form of a written paper of 9000-18000 characters. 5) Points required for passing the course successfully can be gained in two ways only: for class participation and contribution to discussions (up to 4 points every class, i.e. up to 40 points for the whole course, a minimum requirement of 20 points) and for class presentations, including those in written form (up to 60 points for one presentation, if presenting more than once, an average is used).
learning_outcomes: the students: - will be able to orient themselves in economic texts (both popular and professional) and to interpret and assess them correctly; - will be able to express their ideas in both written and oral form in a limited space and in a prescribed and structured way, thus reinforcing the correct usage of economic terms and the economic way of thinking; - will gain a firmer grasp of current and historical, and theoretical and political issues, and will develop their own substantiated position on such issues, which will assist them to choose the topic for their bachelor theses more responsibly.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 40 % Presentation or defence: 60 %
literature: Recommended: Články dle volby cvičícího (zpravidla z www.jstor.org). / Articles of instructor's choice (mostly at www.jstor.org).

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces students to the economic way of thinking about social issues that are seemingly non-economic, but have been fruitfully studied by economists since the 1960’s: Family, sex, crime, health and even religion. The lectures introduce the main ideas and models on each topic; in seminars the students engage in a critical discussion of assigned original articles.
course_content: 1. Economics of life - introduction 2. Marriage and the family - an economic approach, economic issues and applications 3. Matching and dating 4. Marriage and labor markets 5. Fertility and family policy 6. Economics of sexual behaviour 7. Economics and psychology 8. Drugs, alcohol and economics 9. Criminal behaviour 10. Crime control policy 11. Health as human capital 12. Aging 13. Religion
learning_outcomes: the students should - understand the rational forces that shape people’s decisions in various aspects of their lives - be familiar with the basic economic models of family and other non-market behavior - use these models to assess public policies concerning family and other nonmarket behavior
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: AVERETT, Susan L.; HOFFMAN, Saul D. Women and the economy : family, work, and pay. Boston: Addison-Wesley, 2010. ISBN 978-0-321-41094-8. Cooter, R. B., & Ulen, T. (2014). Law and economics. Pearson.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course enables a better understanding of the principles of the labour market. It analyses also its wider social connotations and links to other disciplines.
course_content: 1. Overview of the Labor Market 2. Labor Supply 3. Labor Demand 4. Labor Market Equilibrium 5. Compensating Wage Differentials 6. Human Capital 7. Labor Mobility 8. Labor Market Discrimination 9. Labor Unions and Collective Bargaining 10. Labor Market Contracts and Work Incentives 11. Unemployment 12. Labor Markets in Transition 13. Labor Markets in the EU / Flexicurity
learning_outcomes: - apply economic theory to the labour market - build a model of behaviour and decision-making of various players on the labour market and the impact of its various institutions - explain a role of the state on the labour market - apply models of labour market to real world situations - assess and evaluate various methods of solving labour market problems with respect to their economic efficiency
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 50 %

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is interpretation of the economic nature, mechanism and efficiency of the public sector.
course_content: General Equilibrium Welfare Economics Social Welfare Function Public Policy to Address Externalities Public Goods Publicly Provided Private Goods Inequality and Redistribution Normative Theory of Taxation Tax Incidence and Tax Distortion Optimal Taxation
learning_outcomes: able to - explain the main principles of modern economic theory in the area of the public sector - distinguish between market and non-market process of alocation of resources and coordination of social activities - critically apply the theory of market failure - analyse the efficiency of tax systems and public expenditures
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 40 % Final test: 60 %

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course is an introduction to the problematic of economics of the environment and of the natural resources. The students will learn the role of the economics as a science in the process of solving of the environmental problems with the explanation, why the environmental problem exists (from the economics point of view) and what are possibilities of solving these problems from the point of view of different schools of economic thoughts. Simultaneously the students will use the theoretical background in the discussion about the environmental policy.
course_content: 1) What is the role of the economist in protecting the environment? 2) Why does one pollutes the environment? 3) Value of nature-goods? 4) What is the role in environmental protection property rights to natural goods? 5) Environmental problems can be addressed by agreement between the polluter and polluted? 6) It is the responsibility of environmental protection of the individual or the government? 7) How to force people and businesses to behave considerately to the environment - I.? 8) How to force people and businesses to behave considerately to the environment - II.? 9) solves the environmental problems of intergenerational solidarity and the principles of sustainable development? 10) How the contrary, the impact of environmental regulation on firms? 11) Are an effective tool for global environmental problems, international agreements? 12) What are the macroeconomic context of environmental protection?
learning_outcomes: - explain reasons of chosen environmental problems of different environmental fields; - shortly characterize approaches of leading schools of economic theory towards environmental problems (ecological economics, neoclassical environmental economics, new institutional economics, market approaches towards environmental protection); - apply basics of relevant theories on main practical issues in this particular field; - distinguish between administrative and economic instruments which are applied in environmental policy and shortly characterize their advantages and disadvantages.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Presentation or defence: 20 % Final test: 60 %

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course serves as an introduction to the mechanism of the European Union, the background of its formation, stages of development as well as the current period. It discusses the types of policies executed within the EU, their specifications, the role of institutions involved in their preparation and adoption and decision making processes. It analyzes the economic foundations of the European Union.
course_content: 1. Introduction to the course, basic information about the European Union (EU) 2. History of the European integration process (Part 1) 3. History of the European integration process (Part 2), the EU enlargement process 4. Institutions and legal system of the European Union 5. The main "internal" policies of the EU, the internal market 6. Fiscal policy and the common budget of the EU 7. The concept of monetary union and the Optimum Currency Area Theory 8. European Central Bank and its monetary policy 9. The impact of the 2008-2009 financial crisis, the European debt crisis 10. The main “external” policies of the EU, facts about foreign trade and energy dependence 11. European migrant crisis, Schengen area, the relationship between the UK and the EU 12. Recent crises in the EU, the impact and policies related to the COVID-19 pandemic 13. The EU's position in the world and the future outlook
learning_outcomes: students will be able to - evaluate the importance of the Czech Republic’s membership in the European Union; - describe the mechanism of the EU functions; - explain how an EU policy is adopted; - judge the influence of EU economic policy at a national level; - distinguish between benefits and disadvantages of EU membership; - summarize the history of European integration; -understand an EU topic on a global scale.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 10 % Presentation or defence: 30 % Mid-term test(s): 20 % Final test: 40 %

The European Union : economics and policies. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2007. ISBN 978-0-521-69727-9. STAAB, Andreas. The European Union explained : institutions, actors, global impact. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 2013. ISBN 978-0-253-00972-2. MCCORMICK, John; OLSEN, Jonathan. The European Union : politics and policies. Boulder: Westview Press, 2017. ISBN 978-0-8133-4984-8. Baldwin, R. E., & Wyplosz, C. (2022). The economics of European integration. McGraw Hill.

course_title: Economic and Political History of Europe in the 20th Century (1918-1991)
university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course is focused on history of Europe since the end of the Great War to the break-up of the USSR. The main aim is to show the students main economic and political turning points of the European history in this time period.
course_content: 1. Paris Peace Conference of 1919 and the problems of the postwar Europe - treaties with defeated states, economic consequences of the peace. 2. Economic and political problems of the 1920s - German reparations and attempts to reach collective security. 3. Italian Fascism - rise to power, main problems. The role of B. Mussolini, corporativism, foreign policy. 4. German Nacism - rise to power, main problems. The role of Adolf Hitler, economic and foreihn policy. 5. Russia/Soviet Union (1917-1939) - war communism, New Economic Policy, collectivization, industrialization, political trials. 6. Economic and political problems of the 1930s - the Great Depression and its consequences, policy of appeasement. 7. The Second World War - economic and political aspects, reasons for the defeat of Germany, economic situation in the European countries. 8. Europe in the first years after the Second World War - Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan and its consequences for European states, First Berlin Crisis. 9. Problems of European Integration after the Second World War - ECSC, EEC, EC, EU. 10. The Soviet Bloc after the Second World War - founding of COMECON, Warsaw Pact, causes of the break-up of the Soviet bloc. 11. The 1950s and 1960s in the European politics - economic causes and consequences of the decolonization, economic development of selected European countries (Great Britain, GDR, France). 12. Europe and the détente: a new turn in the economic development, oil crisis (1973, 1979) and their consequences, OSCE. 13. The 1980s in Europen politics: new generation of politicians and new political style: M. Thatcher, Helmut Kohl and François Mitterrand.
learning_outcomes: - describe the most important events of economic and political history of Europe after the Great War (until the break-up of the USSR); - analyze causes and consequences of the rise of authoritarian and totalitarian regimes in Europe in the interwar period; - understand economic and political consequences of the two world wars; - explain causes and course of the European integration process; - understand causes of the break-up of the Soviet Bloc.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 50 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: BEREND, T. Iván. An economic history of twentieth-century Europe : economic regimes from laissez-faire to globalization. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2016. ISBN 978-1-107-13642-7. BEREND, T. Iván. History of European integration : a new perspective. London: Routledge, 2016. ISBN 978-1-138-65490-7. BROADBERRY, S. N.; O'ROURKE, Kevin H. The Cambridge economic history of modern Europe. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2010. ISBN 978-0-521-70838-8. CENTRE FOR ECONOMIC POLICY RESEARCH; CRAFTS, N. F. R.; TONIOLO, Gianni (ed.). Economic growth in Europe since 1945. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1996. ISBN 0-521-49964-X. Recommended: BEREND, T. Iván. university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The subject enables deeper understanding of economic development of the world from the beginning of early capitalism to the beginning of the 21st century. In its context students will familiarize with basic tendencies of economic and political development of the Czech lands and Czechoslovakia in the 19th and the 20th centuries.
course_content: 1. Lecture Importance of economic history. The main features of the early-modern economy. Selected theories of capitalism. General economic context of overseas discoveries. 1. Seminar Economic development of Portugal, Spain, England, and the Netherlands in the 16th and 17th centuries. 2. Lecture Emergence and gradual increase of importance of manufactories, their typology. Mercantilism, cameralism. Protoindustrialization. 2. Seminar Economic development of France and Prussia at the onset of capitalism. 3. Lecture Agricultural revolution, industrial revolution. Industrial revolution vs. industrialization. The course of the industrial revolution in Great Britain. 3. Seminar Course of the industrial revolution in the USA (War of Independence, Civil War, the economic advent in the late 19th century). 4. Lecture World economy, globalization, protectionism in the second half of the 19th century. Impacts of the industrial revolution in Great Britain and France at the end of the 19th century. 4. Seminar The course of the industrial revolution in united Germany, Tsarist Russia (after 1880), Japan. 5. Lecture The First World War, its impacts on the world economy. Postwar international and economic development before 1921. 5. Seminar Comparison of economic development of Great Britain and Germany (1918-1932 emphasized) in the interwar period. 6. Lecture Economic development of the USA in the 1920s. Great Depression in the USA. 6. Seminar Economic development of Czechoslovakia (1918-1945). 7. Lecture Economic policies of totalitarian regimes in the interwar times. Economic development of Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy. 7. Seminar Economic development of the Soviet Union (1917-1945). 8. Lecture The Second world war. War economy of Nazi Germany. Economic exploitation of occupied areas. 8. Seminar Formation of the Soviet Bloc. Economic development of the Soviet Union and Eastern Bloc countries in the 1950s and 1960s. 9. Lecture Postwar reconstruction in Europe, the beginnings of international and European integration, the beginnings of the welfare state. Establishment and functioning of the Bretton Woods monetary system. 9. Seminar Process of economic integration and military and political cooperation of Western European countries in broader and global context. 10. Lecture Economic boom in Western Europe in general and in the USA in the 1950s and 1960s. 10. Seminar Economic miracle of the Federal Republic of Germany in comparison with the economic development of Great Britain. 11. Lecture Oil shocks and their impacts on the market economies in the 1970s and 1980s (the USA and Great Britain emphasized). 11. Seminar Economic backwardness of the Soviet Union and the Soviet bloc countries in the 1970s and 1980s. 12. Lecture Development of Japanese economy in the second half of the 20th century. 12. Seminar Chinese economy in the second half of the 20th century. 13. Lecture Development of Czechoslovak economy (1945-1989). 13. Seminar Principles of the transformation process in Czechoslovakia, re-united Germany, Russia, and other post-socialist countries.
learning_outcomes: students would be able to: - evaluate the impacts of overseas expansion on the world economy; - compare the process of industrial revolution in Great Britain, USA, Germany, Russia; - evaluate the principal turning points of economic and political development of Czechoslovakia in the 20th century; - judge the importance of the two world wars on the subsequent economic development; - define the characteristics of war economy on the example of Russian Civil War, First World War and Second World War; - describe the characteristics of the central planned economy on the case of the Soviet Union and afterwar Eastern Bloc; - define the main principals of afterwar economic miracle in Western Europe; - describe the economic causes of the collapse of the Eastern Bloc and the main problems of the transformation of the post-communist countries.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Mid-term test(s): 40 % Final test: 40 %

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course clarifies effects of specific macroeconomic policies in three- and four-sector models (open and closed economy, respectively), using basic economic modelling. Also, more advanced unemployment and inflation analysis is employed, and the most common economic growth models are presented.
course_content: 1. Macroeconomic aggregates and their interdependencies: domestic product and its measurement, public finances, aggregate expenditures, aggregate savings and investment. 2. Consumption and Savings: Keynesian consumption function, Inter-temporal Choice, Life-Cycle Hypothesis, Permanent Income Hypothesis, investment function, loanable funds market, foreign investment and net export function. 3. Money and Money Market; Inflation: money demand, money creation, money market equilibrium, transmission mechanism, Quantitative Theory of Money, neutrality of money; inflation, its measurement, causes and main anti-inflationary policies. 4. External Balance: balance of payments, net export, real and nominal exchange rate, purchasing power parity theory, fixed exchange rate regime. 5 Labour Market: production function and demand for labour, natural rate of unemployment 6. Economic Growth: neoclassical model of growth, steady state, population growth, exogenous technological progress, endogenous growth theory, institutions and their impact on economic growth. 7. Aggregate Demand in a Three-Sector Economy (IS-LM): product market equilibrium, Keynesian Cross and the IS curve, money market equilibrium and the LM curve, government and its aggregate demand stimulation; multiplication effect and crowding-out effect; monetary stimulation of aggregate demand, real money balances effect, aggregate demand 8. Aggregate Demand in an Open Economy: Mundell-Fleming Model (IS-LM-BP): product market equilibrium, money market equilibrium, demand stimulation under floating exchange rate regime, aggregate demand curve. 9. Aggregate Supply and the AS-AD Model: potential product and classical aggregate supply, sticky wages, price level misperception, imperfect price information, shifts of the AS and AD curves. 10. Phillips Curve, Okun´s Law, demand-pull inflation; inflation expectations, inflation inertia, cost-push inflation. 11. Business Cycles: history and empirical evidence, monetary cycles, real business cycle theory, new keynesians vs. new classics. 12. Exchange Rate in the Short Run: interest rate, relation between interest rate parity and PPP, exchange rate overshooting model, Czech monetary crisis and the role of CB. 13. Monetary Policy: goals and tools, central bank independence, activist monetary policy, rules vs. discretion in monetary policy, rational expectations and policy ineffectiveness, disinflationary policy and the sacrifice ratio, stable exchange rate regime, Golden Rule of money supply, inflation targeting. 14. European Monetary Union convergence process: Optimal Currency Area Theory, costs and benefits of joining the EMU, real and nominal convergence, Balassa-Samuelson Theorem, costs of economic policy aimed at Euro currency adoption. 15. Public Finances: budget deficit and aggregate demand - traditional theory, Barro-Ricardo Hypothesis, taxes and economic growth, public debt.
learning_outcomes: - describe functioning of economic system in its complexity - explain problems of economic growth - discuss the possibilities and limits of individual theories - employ basic quantitative techniques to model aggregate economic phenomena - describe specific problems associated with openness and mutual inter-connectedness of individual economies
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Final test: 80 %
literature: Basic: Dornbusch, R., Fischer, S., & Startz, R. (2001). Macroeconomics. McGraw-Hill. Mankiw, N. G. (2006). Macroeconomics. Worth Publishers. Holman, R. (2004). Makroekonomie : středně pokročilý kurz. C.H. Beck.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course focuses on supplying students with the basic mathematical concepts and tools used in economics. It aims at practical use.
course_content: 1) Set and logical operations, operations with numerical and variable expressions, solution of elementary equations and inequalities. 2) Graphs of linear, quadratic and linear fractional functions. 3) Powers, roots, exponential, logarithm. 4) Finite and infinite sequences, sequence limits, limit arithmetic, infinite series. 5) Functions of one variable, superposition of functions, domain, domain of values, inverse functions, limits of functions. 6) Derivation of a function, rules for calculation of derivatives, calculation of a tangent. 7) L'Hospital's rule, monotonicity, stationary points, local and global extremes. 8) Even and odd functions, asymptotes, second derivation and convexity/concavity. 9) Complete examination of the course of function. 10) Mid-term test. 11) Functions of several variables, partial derivatives, stationary points, Jacobi matrices. 12) Optimization problems, bounded extrema, substitution method (for two variables), Lagrange multiplier method, meaning of multiplier. 13) Applications in economics: concept of limit value, differential and total differential, Taylor polynomials and approximate calculations using them, implicit functions and their derivatives, elasticity, homogeneity of functions, Cobb-Douglas production function.
learning_outcomes: - calculate derivatives of functions of one variable composed of elementary functions, use it to calculate the tangent to a function at a point, - calculate the limits of sequence and function, i.a. using L'Hospital's rule, - use the first and second derivatives to determine the behavior of a function (monotonicity, locational extrema, convexity, concavity), investigate the course of a function and sketch its graph, - determine bounded extrema of functions of two variables using Lagrange multipliers and substitution method.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 40 % Final test: 60 %
literature: Basic: Klůfa, J., & Kaspříková, N. (2013). Mathematics for economic universities (1st ed.). Ekopress. Chiang, A. C., & Wainwright, K. (2005). Fundamental methods of mathematical economics. McGraw-Hill. Recommended: PEMBERTON, Malcolm; RAU, Nicholas. Mathematics for economists: An introductory textbook, Manchester, MUP, 2015, 978-1784991487

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course is focused on an analysis of consumer and producer choice (both in markets for goods and services and in factor markets) using intermediate analytical tools; decision under risk and uncertainty; general equilibrium; market failures and government interventions.
course_content: The subject and method of economics Theory of the Consumer: consumer substitution, budget constraint, cardinal and ordinal approach. Demand: demand curve, substitution and income effects, price elasticity, Engel´s curve, income and cross-elasticity of demand, consumer surplus. Trade-off between consumption and leisure. Trade-off between current and future consumption: time preference, optimal combination of current and future consumption. Decision under uncertainty and risk: expected income, expected utility, risk-lowering information, insurance against risk, diversification. Theory of the firm: transaction costs and the nature of the firm; goals of a firm; principal-agent problem; the production function and costs: short run, long run; technical substution. Social costs and supply: social costs, supply in the short and long runs. Exchange: production possibility frontier, optimal combination of production, exchange based on differing consumption preferences, exchange based on differing production possibilities: absolute and comparative advantage, international trade and the Heckscher-Ohlin theory, migration, protectionism. Market equilibrium and market price; benefits of exhange, changes in equilibrium, state interventions. Imperfect markets: price-taker and price-maker, price creation on an imperfect market, price discrimination, monopolistic competition, oligopoly (Cournot, Bertrand models), cartel, Nash equilibrium, open and regulated markets. Monopoly and competition: monopolist’s equilibrium, discriminating monopoly, ownership of a unique factor, monopoly and competition as a process, natural monopoly, network effect. Labour markets: production function and labour demand, market supply of labour, equilibrium of the labour market, trade unions, minimum wages, discrimination on labour markets, unemployment, workplace hazards and compensating wage differentials. Capital and interest: use of and investment in capital, investment opportunities and willingness to save, loanable funds market, nominal and real interest rate, allocation of capital, human capital. General equilibrium and efficiency: general equilibrium, production efficiency, allocation efficiency, efficient combination of goods, compensation payments. Externalities and free goods: social and private costs and benefits, taxes and subsidies to suppress externalities, property rights and the Coase theorem, free goods. Public goods and public choice: non-excludability and the free-rider problem, non-rivalry, public choice: the political market, median voter theorem, rational ignorance, interest groups. Law and economics: crime and punishment, tort, accidents.
learning_outcomes: - demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the most important aspects of consumer theory and theory of the firm - describe the behavior of economic agents, functioning of markets and the whole market system - describe the equilibrium of system of markets as a whole - solve microeconomic problems - use quantitative techniques at an intermediate level
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 40 % Final test: 60 %
literature: Basic: NICHOLSON, Walter; SNYDER, Christopher M. Intermediate microeconomics and its application. Mason: South-Western Cengage Learning, 2010. ISBN 978-0-324-59910-7. Recommended: Varian, H. R. (1990). Intermediate microeconomics : a modern approach. W.W. Norton. Nicholson, W. (2002). Microeconomic theory : basic principles and extensions. South-Western/Thomson Learning. Holman, R. (2007). Mikroekonomie : středně pokročilý kurz. C.H. Beck.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course explains how firms compete with each other, how they asquire and maintain market power. It models strategies of firms on imperfect markets. The course combines modern microeconomic theory approach together with empirical evidence and practical demonstrations.
course_content: 1) Course introduction: What, how and why? Strategies (conduct) of firms, competition laws 2) Firm and market: Costs, market structure and efficiency : 3) From competition to monopoly: Barriers to entry, market power and welfare 4) Monopoly and price discrimination 5) Product differentiation, strategic conduct of firms and anti-trust laws 6) Oligopoly markets: Strategic interaction and game theory 7) Oligopoly models: Bertrand model, tacit collusion 9) Cartels: Dominant strategy, prisoner dilemma, prohibition of anti-competitive agreements 10) Mergers: Market delineation, market concentration and approval of mergers 11) Non-price competition: Advertisement and information, unfair competition 12) Non-price competition: R&D expenditures, innovation and patents 13) State and market power: Anti-trust, regulation and economic analysis
learning_outcomes: - recognize and describe various market structures and firm strategies - measure market power and apply basic concepts of economic analysis of market structures - understand debates concerning competition policy
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 10 % Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 60 %
literature: Basic: NORMAN, George; PEPALL, Lynne; RICHARDS, Dan. Industrial organization : contemporary theory and empirical applications. Malden: Blackwell Publishing, 2008. ISBN 978-1-4051-7632-3. university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is an introduction into scientific way of thinking, working and discussion. It focuses on presenting the basic scientific methods used in scientific work. As part of the course, students are introduced to the essence and meaning of an education and the role of science and ethics in truth seeking. The course is highly interactive - students’ presence and involvement in activities during lessons is essential.
course_content: 1. Principles of effective study and the role of an intellectual in the society. Scheduling and time management. Meaning of education and science, ethics of scientific work and the role of elites in society. 2. Basic science tools: hypothesis (model) and its falsification, causality and correlation, positive and normative theories. Advantages, disadvantages and limits of using theoretical, experimental and empirical methods. Methodology of natural and social sciences. 3. Information sources and how to determinate their trustworthiness. Introduction to citation indexes, full-text databases and basic concepts of scientometrics, overview of available catalogues. Risks and advantages of other information sources. 4. Work with references, citation norms and ethics, explanation and practical demonstration of using citation norms, bibliography usage. Introduction to working with citation managers. 5. Importance of scientific skepticism and necessity of information verification. Principles of critical thinking. Introduction to principles of logic and forms of logical illusions. Critical reading of chosen scientific text. 6. Basic spreadsheet skills (MS Excel), graph creation. Preparing documents in word processor (MS Word), emphasis on separation of content, structure and look using formatting. Formatting and look of scientific papers and qualification works at UEP. 7. The art and rules of presentation, scientific discussion and the rule of criticism. Structure and nature of lecture/presentation. Introduction to rhetoric. 8. Principles of writing scientific paper (procedure, optimal structure, contents criteria), most common mistakes. Styles of scientific writing. Types of texts and spelling rules. Importance of distributed intelligence (cooperation with colleagues, tutors, teachers, experts). Interdisciplinarity of scientific fields. 9. Presentation of workshop paper and opponent review in moderated discussion.
learning_outcomes: - identify a problem; - propose a hypothesis; - find and critically assess available information (including academic resources in English); - formulate their opinion by means of a written research paper, present it and defend it in a discussion.
assessment_methods: Term paper: 40 % Presentation or defence: 20 % Mid-term test(s): 20 % Preparation of the comments (reviews).: 20 %

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: During lectures students will meet with subject and methods of the socio-economic geography, which provides them to understand development, present situation and principal trends in distribution of economic and other socio-economic activities at various regional levels.
course_content: Program: - Structure and content of socio-economic geography. Development of thoughts of spatial society organization. Geographic environment. - Methods and techniques of socio-economic geography - basic techniques, quantitative methods, sample research etc. Methods and techniques of economic-cartographic analyses. - Theory of region. Development of thoughts of region conception. Structure of region - homogenous and heterogeneous region. Order and hierarchy of regions. Border of regions. Regionalization. Typology of regions, economic region, political and administrative region. - Diffusion innovation theory. Basic types, growth of innovation during time period, innovation barriers. - Spatial population differentiation and general characteristics of territorial inhabitant concentration. Spatial marks of population and migration, development of the world population and its selected structural features. - Structure of settlement and basic components of settlement. Settlement structure development, effects of socio-economic and natural conditions on formation of settlement. Classification of municipalities. Rural and transitional seats. Formation and development of towns, functions of towns. Formation of spatial settlement systems. - General features of geography of the world economy. Economic organization and primary activity, natural sources and economic growth. - Patterns of spatial agriculture organization. Basic characteristics of present world agriculture. - Regional structures of industry, industrial nodal points and regions, branch structure of industry, localization factors, concentration and dislocation of industry. Development and present trends of world industrial production. - Regional structures of tourism and recreation. Individual recreation as a phenomenon of the end of millennium and its economic and regional importance. International tourism. - Transport and communications nodal points and regions, Classification and sorts of transport and communications networks. Present trends in the world transport. - Regional structures of services and trade. Role of market at forming of spatial service systems. Territorial and commodity structure of the world trade. - Center periphery system of the world. Socioeconomic overview of Europe, North America and Latin America. Future of Asia- Pacific Region, development countries and the world geopolitical situation. - Position of the Czech Republic in the world economy. - Integrating, disintegrating and globalization processes in present world. Spatial look at world economy and levels of economic maturity. - European Union and its institutional base, the EU authorities, the EU functioning, regions.
learning_outcomes: - work with patterns and context in real functioning socioeconomic system, including inter- action relations among nature, society and economic processes - use obtained knowledge from the field of political geography, geography of population and settlement, economic geography including present picture of the states in the world and important integrating processes - apply basic research methods and techniques of socioeconomic geography in studying follow-up subjects - explain position of the Czech Republic in current world economy - explain basic regional distribution of sectors of national economy (agriculture, industry, services) in the Czech Republic - describe basic integration groupings in Europe - explain basic integration and desintegration processes in developing parts of the world
assessment_methods: Term paper: 15 % Presentation or defence: 15 % Final test: 30 % Final oral exam: 40 %
