university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: Accounting as an information tool for the decision-making of an entity’s owners, managers, investors in capital markets, creditors and other users of financial statements. Fundamental elements of financial statements and their content. Impacts of transactions and events on financial position and performance. European and worldwide regulation and harmonisation of accounting.
course_content: Introduction to accounting: the nature, principles of financial accounting, characteristics of accounting. Objectives of financial accounting, scope of financial reporting, users of financial statements. Accounting categories and definitions, characteristics of accounting elements, accounting cycle, standardization of financial reporting. Elements of accounting method: balance sheet equation, nature of accounts, double-entry and duality concept. Documentation, controls, measurement; transaction analysis. Accounting cycle. Income statement accounts: expenditures and receipts, expense and revenue, classification of expenses by nature and by function. Business models: a trade company vs. a manufacturer. Conceptual framework. Features of world harmonization process. EU Directives, EU, US GAAP and IFRS. Non-current assets: Definition, classification, measurement, depreciation and amortisation. Inventory: definition, classification, measurement. Investments: definition, classification, measurement, presentation, transactions. Receivables and revenues: recognition principle, measurement, accruals and contingent assets. Liabilities and provisions: classification, measurement, accruals and deferrals, contingent liabilities. Equity: classification, features of different types of companies, capital contribution and distribution, Statements of Changes in Equity, Statement of Comprehensive Income. Cash flow statement. Notes to financial statements. Audit. Issuing of financial statements. Annual Report.
learning_outcomes: - understand the content of financial statements for external users; - define and explain the nature of fundamental elements of financial statements; - record the basic economic transactions on the face of financial statements; - master the accounting cycle, including double-entry; - prepare an elementary set of financial statements; - describe the main features of accounting regulation and harmonisation both in national and international context.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 10 % Mid-term test(s): 15 % Final test: 60 % case studies: 15 %
literature: Basic: Zárybnická Žárová, M., Peprníčková, M., Vácha, P., Svitlík, J., & Čiperová, L. (2022). Essentials of financial accounting for non-specialists (2nd ed.). Nakladatelství Oeconomica. Collins, B., & McKeith, J. (2010). Financial accounting and reporting. McGraw-Hill Higher Education. Recommended: ASSOCIATION OF CHARTERED CERTIFIED ACCOUNTANTS (VELKÁ BRITÁNIE). ACCA, complete text : December 2011, June 2012 and December 2012 exam sittings. Paper F3 (INT/UK), Financial accounting (FA). Wokingham: Kaplan Publishing, 2011. ISBN 978-0-85732-278-4.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is designed to provide an elementary but comprehensive introduction to the practice of econometrics. Even though essential theory will be developed in class, the primary emphasis of the course will be on a sequence of specific practical problems involving the specification, estimation and interpretation of econometric models with the aid of econometric software.
course_content: - The Nature of Econometrics and Economic Data. - Linear Regression Model. Estimation (using econometric software), interpretation, properties of estimates; multicollinearity and stability. - Identification Issues. Self-selection, selection bias, identification strategies, experimental design. Case study: returns to education. - Statistic Inference in Linear Regression Models. Hypothesis testing in econometric software. - Economics and Econometrics. Working economic hypotheses into econometric models, economic verification of estimates. - Specification Issues. Omitted variables, functional forms of regression models and their interpretation, linear coefficient restrictions. Case study: Cobb-Douglas production function. - Qualitative Information. Dummy variables and their interactions, difference-in-differences estimator. Case study: inter-industry and gender wage differentials. - Generalized Linear Regression Models. Heteroskedasticity, autocorrelation.
learning_outcomes: the students will be provided with the theoretical tools and practical experience necessary to carry out applied econometric research.
assessment_methods: Term paper: 30 % Mid-term test(s): 20 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: WOOLDRIDGE, Jeffrey M. Introductory econometrics : a modern approach. [S.l.]: South-Western Cengage Learning, 2009. ISBN 978-0-324-78890-7. Recommended: Gujarati, D. N. (2003). Basic econometrics. McGraw-Hill. Lejnarová, Š., Ráčková, A., & Zouhar, J. (2009). Základy ekonometrie v příkladech. Oeconomica. Hušek, R. (2009). Aplikovaná ekonometrie : teorie a praxe. Oeconomica. Hebák, P., Hustopecký, J., Jarošová, E., & Pecáková, I. (2007). Vícerozměrné statistické metody [1] (2nd ed.). Informatorium. Hebák, P., Hustopecký, J., & Malá, I. (2005). Vícerozměrné statistické metody. [2]. Informatorium. Lecture notes, slides, and other materials available online.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course provides a general introduction into philosophy - in the form of an insight into the following philosophical disciplines: ontology, epistemology, ethics, philosophical anthropology, and political philosophy; this is combined with a basic treatment of human knowledge in general and with the exposition of some questions of the methodology of scientific knowledge. The most important philosophical conceptions, themes and motives are explained with the aid of reading and interpreting classical philosophical texts. The aim is: A) The course is aimed at giving the students such a degree of philosophical cultivation which will enable them to understand the basic problems of philosophy and methodology of science (mainly at the elementary level) and to orient themselves in philosophical problems of both human knowledge in general and scientific knowledge. B) The course makes students familiar with the fundamental topics of philosophical and philosophico-political thought and with the main stages of their historical development. C) The course introduces the students into the basic philosophical conceptions including philosophical and logical foundations of social sciences and their methodology.
course_content: The course is focused on the fundamental ideas of Western philosophical thinking. The explications proceed in systematic way which is not limited only to a particular philosophical standpoint; thus, any student is given a possibility to choose his/her own philosophical orientation. It is the explication the most important conceptual instruments of the philosophical reflections of the world, human knowledge and phenomenon of man, which is stressed prevalently. The main attention is paid to the elementary philosophical categories as, for example, category of time, space, nature, mind, society, history, politics, freedom etc.; nevertheless, the explications of methodological approaches to scientific knowledge of social and economic reality are an integral part of the course, too. Any single seminar has always one main theme, i.e., some philosophical ideas of the following important Western philosophers: Plato, Aristotle, Augustinus Aurelius, Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau, Kant, Hegel, Marx, Hayek, Popper, Rawls. [The assortment of philosophers is variable.]
learning_outcomes: -describe the specific character of the philosophical approach to reality; -summarize an overview of the basic streams and methods of philosophical thought; -interpret critically philosophical texts, especially in the field of political philosophy.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 30 % Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 40 %

course_title: Introduction to Economic and Social Policy
university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: Students will become familiar with all the main economic policies and their fundamental characteristics, instruments, and objectives. The course will point out the relationships between economic policies, actors, objectives, and instruments in the historical and current contexts.
course_content: 1. Introduction, General Theoretical Concepts, Objectives and Instruments of Economic Policy, Efficiency of Economic Policy 2. Economic Policy and Social Decision-Making System, Bureaucracy, Interest Groups, and Corruption 3. Economic Policymakers, Process of Economic Policy, Main Conceptual Approaches to Economic Policy 4. Limits of Economic Policy, Market and Government Failures, Political Cycles 5. History of Economic Policy (Part I) - Theoretical and Practical Approaches 6. History of Economic Policy (Part II) - Economic Policies of Selected Countries 7. Capitalism, Poverty, Social Policy, Redistributive Policy 8. Fiscal Policy and Public Finance 9. Central Banks, Monetary Policy and Macroprudential Policy 10. External Economic Policy, International Economic Integration, Balance of Payments, Trade Policy 11. Exchange Rate Systems and Exchange Rate Policy, Problematics of Foreign Direct Investment 12. Other Types of Economic Policies (Part I) 13. Other Types of Economic Policies (Part II), Economic Transformation of Czech Economy After 1989, Recent Economic Crises, Current Economic Policy Mainstream and Effects of Long-Term Expansionary Economic Policies
learning_outcomes: - develop and apply basic pieces of theoretical knowledge regarding the national economy; - summarize elementary goals of economic and social policy; - distinguish between different economic-political conceptions from market-conformal to interventional; - evaluate the role of public institutions in economic-political decision making; - describe elementary functions of monetary and fiscal policy and judge its effectiveness in different economic situtations; - distinguish between basic instrument and principles of social policy; - identify economic impacts of different degrees of economic integration and impacts of eonomic interventions of European union.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Final test: 80 %
literature: Basic: BÉNASSY-QUÉRÉ, Agnès; COEURÉ, Benoît; JACQUET, Pierre; PISANI-FERRY, Jean. Economic policy : theory and practice. New York: Oxford University Press, 2019. ISBN 978-0-19-091210-9. university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces students to the main legal systems, in particular the continental and common law legal systems. It also clarifies the hierarchy of sources of law with respect to national and international laws as well as the law of the European Union. The course surveys the functioning of the EU institutions and EU Law, including competition law. Also, it deals with practical aspects related to starting a business, such as the setting up business corporations, the law of contracts, labor law and corporate social responsibility (CSR).
course_content: 1.Concept of Law, Sources of International, EU and National Law, Legal Systems (allowance 4/0) 2.Doing Business in the Czech Republic (allowance 4/0) 3.International Public Law (allowance 4/0) 4.EU Institutions (allowance 4/0) 5.EU Law (allowance 4/0) 6.Czech Constitutional Law and the Protection of Human Rights in the Czech Rep. and the EU (allowance 4/0) 7.Labor Law (allowance 4/0) 8.Legal liability (allowance 4/0) 9.Competition Law (allowance 4/0) 10.Introduction to the Law of Contracts (allowance 4/0) 11.Introduction to Corporate Law (allowance 4/0) 12.Procedural law - Litigation and ADR (allowance 4/0) 13.Business Ethics and Corporate Social Responsibility (allowance 4/0)
learning_outcomes: Students will be able to - explain the role of law in society - enumerate different types of legal norm - describe the law making process - interpret legal texts - express the essence of contracts and corporate law - decide which legal norm will be applied
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s) 50 %: 0 % Final test 50 %: 0 % Total 100 %: 0 %

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces students to basic concepts in Law and Economics. The basic premise of Law and Economics is that law shapes the incentives that people and firms face in many aspects of their market and non-market behavior: whether to buy a house, what to write in a contract, how carefully to drive a car, or whether to kill an accidental witness (a problem faced occasionally by criminals). The course employs a standard toolbox of economic theory to analyze how legal rules affect the behavior of people and firms, how they affect market outcomes, and how they compare in terms of economic efficiency. Among other things, the course will compare selected Continental and American legal concepts and explore the new Czech Civil Code from an economic perspective.
course_content: 1.Introduction: law and efficiency, legal rules, precedents, norms 2.Externalities and Coase theorem 3.Nuisance law, the reach and limits of legal regulation 4.Property 5.Intellectual property 6.Midterm 7.Contracts: formation and breach 8.Contracts: asymmetric information and risk allocation 9.Tort law 10.Economics of crime: theory of optimal punishment 11.Topics in economics of crime 12.Procedure: criminal and civil 13.Legislative process and regulatory impact assessment
learning_outcomes: understand the key concepts (e.g., how law affects incentives, the Coase theorem, allocation of risk in contracts, economics of liability, optimal punishment, etc) apply those concepts to predict the effects of legal rules on economic outcomes compare the efficiency properties of alternative legal rules
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 40 % Final test: 60 %

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: This course provides the student with the basic toolset of a modern approach to strategic issues in agents‘ behaviour, called Game Theory.
course_content: 1) What is game theory about? How does it differ from traditional analysis? Applications of game theory Basic concepts: actions, preferences, rational choice, strategic games, extensive and normal forms, strategies, games with and without dominant strategies, iterative elimination of strictly dominated strategies --------------Static games------------------ 2) Nash equilibrium definition, beliefs, best response function, strictly and weakly dominated actions, examples 3-4) Mixed strategy equilibrium definition, expected payoffs, best response function, strictly and weakly dominated actions, pure equilibria, existence, formation of player’s beliefs, randomisation, examples, trembling hand perfection -----------------Dynamic games ------------------------ 5-6) Subgame perfect equilibrium analysis of extensive-form games with perfect information, players and information sets, backward induction, subgame perfection, forward induction, applications and examples, bargaining and negotiations, ultimatum game, centipede game 7) Midterm 8-9) Games of complete but imperfect information applications and examples. -------------Static games------------------ 10-11) Bayesian Nash equilibrium static games of incomplete information, examples, normal form representation of bayesian games, applications - auction, double auction -----------------Dynamic games ------------------------ 12-13) Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium pooling and separating equilibrium, finding PBE in signaling games, applications - job market signaling, cheap talk games
learning_outcomes: be in the context of simultaneous as well as dynamic games and repeated games, all of various degrees of information available to the players able to - formulate interdependence of economic agents’ actions in a game-form - solve the game - provide criticism of the applicability of such approach to the real world - from many applications, advise designers of an institution how to optimally set the incentives of economic agents involved
assessment_methods: Term paper: 10 % Mid-term test(s): 25 % Final test: 55 % Homeworks: 10 %
literature: Basic: Osborne, M. J. (2004). An introduction to game theory. Oxford University Press. Recommended: Robert Gibbons, A Primer in Game Theory, 1992

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to introduce students to the economic dimensions of global environmental policy, sustainable development, and international cooperation in environmental protection. The course focuses on key instruments of environmental economics, the structure of global environmental governance including the role of the United Nations (especially UNEP), international environmental agreements, market failures in green innovation, and the economic impacts of climate change and natural capital loss. Students will explore specific policies such as carbon pricing, emissions trading, circular economy strategies, and the transition to green technologies. In the seminar section, students will develop skills to analyze environmental problems using economic tools and prepare for careers in environmental policy, public administration, international organizations, or ESG-related roles in the private sector.
course_content: 1. Dimensions of Sustainable Development: Global Strategies and Policy Instruments 2. Global Environmental Governance: The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP): Impact on states 3. International Environmental Agreements and their implications and impact on states 4. Plastics and Their Global Impact: Policies, Sustainability, and Solutions 5. Fossil Fuels in the Global Economy: Oil, Gas, Coal and Their Economic and Environmental Trade-Offs 6.Circular Economy and Resource Efficiency: Economic Models and Policy Challenges 7. Carbon-Pricing Mechanisms 8. The Economics of Natural Capital and Ecosystem Services 9. Green Technology Innovation and Market Failures 10. Economy of Environmental Catastrophes and Tipping Points 11. Climate Economy Under Deep Uncertainty: Fat Tails and Extreme Risks 12. The Green Paradox: When Climate Policies Backfire
learning_outcomes: - understand key economic concepts and instruments related to environmental protection, sustainable development, and climate change; - identify and explain the role of major international environmental institutions and agreements and their impact on national economic policies, including those of the Czech Republic; - analyze the effects of environmental policies such as carbon taxes, emissions trading systems, and green technology subsidies on economies and societies; - discuss market failures in the context of natural capital, ecosystem services, and environmental innovation; - evaluate the economic implications of environmental catastrophes, tipping points, and extreme climate risks; - interpret the ESG framework and understand its significance for both the public and private sectors; - propose solutions to environmental challenges using economic tools with attention to efficiency and long-term sustainability.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 25 % Term paper: 13 % Presentation or defence: 12 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: LEWIS, Lynne; TIETENBERG, Thomas H. Environmental and natural resource economics. New York: Routledge, Taylor & Francis Group, 2018. ISBN 978-1-138-63229-5. Šauer, P. (2007). Introduction to environmental economics and policy with economic lab experiments and class exercises. Nakladatelství a vydavatelství litomyšlského semináře. ŠAUER, Petr; ŠAUEROVÁ, Jana; VEJCHODSKÁ, Eliška (ed.). Environmental Economics, Policy and International Relations. Prague: Litomysl Seminar Publishing, 2011. 132 p. ISBN 978-80-86709-17-8. Recommended: GATES, Bill. How to avoid a climate disaster: the solutions we have and the breakthroughs we need. New York: Alfred A. Knopf, 2021. ISBN 978-0-593-08185-3. SACHS, Jeffrey D. The age of sustainable development. New York: Columbia University Press, 2015. ISBN 978-0-231-17315-6. SMIL, Vaclav. How the world really works: the science behind how we got here and where we're going. New York: Viking, 2022. ISBN 978-0-593-29706-2. TREBECK, Katherine; WILLIAMS, Jeremy. The economics of arrival: ideas for a grown-up economy. Bristol: Policy Press, 2019. ISBN 978-1-4473-3726-3. WAGNER, Gernot a WEITZMAN, Martin L. Climate shock: the economic consequences of a hotter planet. Princeton; Oxford: Princeton University Press, 2015. ISBN 978-0-691-17132-6.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course provides students with the basic knowledge of and proficiency in taxation and social security contributions in the Czech Republic in the extent necessary for decision-making.
course_content: 1.Přehled o zdanění včetně pojistného sociálního pojištění v podmínkách České republiky. (allowance 0/0)
learning_outcomes: students will be able to: - take into account the tax optimization during decision-making; - recognize situations requiring or enabling the tax optimisation; - assess macroeconomic connection of the taxation in the Czech Republic and its development such as tax price of labour and capital; - estimate tax obligations of an entrepreneurial subject.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 25 % Mid-term test(s): 25 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: Vančurová, A., & Zídková, H. (2022). Daňový systém ČR 2022 (1st ed.). Wolters Kluwer ČR. Recommended: texty, domácí úkoly a testy v Moodle

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the subject is to familiarize students with the essence of financial management (management of long-term assets, working capital and sources of financing). The course is aimed at mastering the issues necessary for making both operational and strategic financial decisions. At the same time, the course should lead to the acquisition of practical habits in analyzing financial statements, evaluating the management of the company and evaluating investment projects and financial investments. In the seminars, case studies are solved with the help of Excel, publicly available sources and some corporate databases. It is assumed that students already have a basic knowledge of accounting.
course_content: 1. Introduction to corporate finance. Financial goals of the company. Tasks and principles of financial management. Business life cycle. 2. Financial statements from the point of view of the financial director. Accounting versus economic interpretation of statements. 3. The external environment of the enterprise - customers, suppliers, competitors, the public, creators and guarantors of rules. The impact of taxes on the financial management. Payout policy. 4. Time and risk in the financial management. Effective interest rate, required return, annuity, perpetuity. Types of risks and methods of protection against them. 5. Management of long-term assets and investment decisions. Net present value, internal rate of return, simple payback period, discounted project cost. Stock's present value and total stock return. Bond's present value and yield to maturity. 6. Management of working capital and its components. The money cycle. 7. Cash flow management. Methods of compiling and planning cash flow. Cash-pooling. 8. Corporate Financing - Equity. Issuance of shares, IPO, additional payment outside the share capital. Market capitalization. 9. Corporate Financing - Debt. Bonds, loans, leasing, crowdfunding, subsidies. 10. Corporate Financing - Capital structure and cost of capital. Basic approaches to capital structure optimization. Weighted average cost of capital. 11. Financial analysis. Procedural steps, comparative analysis, ratio indicators and their interpretation. 12. Digital tools for managing corporate finances.
learning_outcomes: - describe the essence of the financial management, - evaluate the management of the company on the basis of financial statements, - name the basic external factors affecting the company and evaluate their impact on the company, - orient yourself in the payout policy rules, - apply and interpret the time and risk factor in financial management tasks, - name methods for evaluating investment projects and financial investments and apply these methods in financial decision-making tasks, - define working capital and evaluate its amount in terms of profitability for the company, - describe the principles and methods of inventory, cash, accounts receivables and short-term liabilities management, - identify and compare different types of capital, - name the problems of optimal capital structure, - estimate the weighted average costs of the business using publicly available information, - prepare a financial analysis of the company at a basic level, - give examples of the use of digital tools in the practice of financial management and describe their benefits for the company, - be able to look up financial information about companies on the Internet and in some company databases.
assessment_methods: Term paper: 15 % Mid-term test(s): 15 % Final test: 70 %
literature: Recommended: Head, A., & Watson, D. (2019). Corporate finance : principles and practice. Pearson. Vernimmen, P. (2014). Corporate finance : theory and practice. John Wiley & Sons. ČIŽINSKÁ, Romana. Základy finančního řízení podniku. Praha: Grada Publishing, 2018. ISBN 978-80-271-0194-8. VOCHOZKA, Marek. Finance podniku: komplexní pojetí. Praha: Grada Publishing, 2021. ISBN 978-80-271-3267-6. OTRUSINOVÁ, Milana; ŠTEKER, Karel. Jak číst účetní výkazy : základy českého účetnictví a výkaznictví. Praha: Grada Publishing, 2021. ISBN 978-80-271-3184-6.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: To acquaint students with formal methodological tools of financial analysis which support systematic use of financial reporting and capital market data for prediction of firm's financial credibility. The course introduces the methods supporting projection of a company´s strategy into its financial management tools (such as financial politics, financial planning, and financial controlling).
course_content: 1. The role of financial analysis and financial planning in financial management. Users of financial analysis and theirs information needs. 2. Sources of information. Accounting statements and theirs publication. Annual reports. Computer support of financial analysis. 3. Methodological elements of financial analysis. Methods of calculation, interpretation and usage of ratios for the purpose of firm's financial health evaluation. 4. Profitability, liquidity, financial stability. 5. Cash flow analysis. Balance sheet of financial sources and financial needs. 6. Multiple models for prediction of firm's financial situation. Factor analysis of profitability, liquidity and financial stability ratios. Balancing of specific operating risk and advantages. Evaluation of financial credibility. 7. Financial health indicators from capital market. Evaluation of financial performance based on market standards. Economic value added. 8. Firm strategy and firm planning. Financial objectives and financial policy. 9. Long term financial planning. Sales prediction. Capital budgeting. Long-term cash flow planning. Maximization of firm's market value criteria. 10. Relationship between operating financial planning (on the yearly basis) and budgeting. Financial controlling. Relationship between financial planning and controlling.
learning_outcomes: - understand financial statements for the purpose of financial analysis; - analyse and interpret data provided with financial statements; - calculation of a variety of key financial ratios in particular fields: profitability, asset turn-over, liquidity, solvency, - obtain information concerning financial situation (performance and financial position) of a company; - assess and quantify associated risk (operating, financial); - estimate entity´s future cash flows; - understand the methodology of financial planning; - create "master budget"; - create long-term and short-term financial plan.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 20 % Final test: 80 %
literature: Basic: BRABENEC, Tomáš. Finanční analýza obchodních korporací. Jesenice: Ekopress, 2022. ISBN 978-80-87865-85-9. Recommended: ALVAREZ, Fernando; FRIDSON, Martin S. Financial statement analysis : a practitioner's guide. Hoboken: John Wiley & Sons, 2011. ISBN 978-0-470-63560-5. ALEXANDER, Jack. Financial planning & analysis and performance management. Hoboken: John Wiley & Sons, 2018. ISBN 978-1-119-49148-4. SAMONAS, Michael. Financial forecasting, analysis, and modelling : a framework for long-term forecasting. Chichester: John Wiley & Sons, 2015. ISBN 978-1-118-92108-1. FABOZZI, Frank J.; PETERSON DRAKE, Pamela. Analysis of financial statements. Hoboken: John Wiley & Sons, 2012. ISBN 978-1-118-29998-2.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to acquaint the audience with the basic questions of financial mathematics, the concepts and mathematical methods used in modern finance, to solve real examples from practice in the field of savings, debt repayment, valuation of selected types of securities and their portfolios, including a focus on ESG.
course_content: 1. Types of interest. Simple interest, present and future value of capital, interest, pre-term and semi-term. Equivalence of interest rates 2. Discounting. Discount rate. Calculation of the average (median) value of the claim, the average payment period and the interest rate 3. Compound interest. Interest earner. Future and present value. Effective interest rate and interest intensity. 4. Future value of the annuity. Short-term, long-term savings. Saver 5. Present value of annuity. Retirement number. Pension temporary, immediate, eternal, deferred. Supplier. Internal rate of return. 6. Mortification. Annuity. Regular and irregular installments, constant annuities, constant amortization. Calculation of amortization and debt balance. 7. Amortization plan, amount of the last installment, depreciation and interest in the selected period. Green and sustainable loans 8. Bonds, their valuation. Nominal and real rate of return. Current value 9. Current and discounted yield. Rendita, realized income 10. Prompt and term yield curves - construction. Valuation of bonds based on the yield curve. The behavior of interest rates. The impact of ESG on yield curves 11. Bond price sensitivity, duration, convexity, portfolio immunization including ESG 12. FX rates - spot rates, cross rates, relationship between spot and forward rates, FX swap and its use. 13. Shares - dividend discount model, impact on ESG
learning_outcomes: - apply the discussed parts of classic financial mathematics (types of interest, savings, pensions) and understand the concept of future and present value with a focus on ESG; - create a table of cash flows and determine the value of investments; - evaluate bonds and shares, including ESG; - find out the sensitivity of bond prices to changes in interest rates (duration, convexity) and immunize given risks against changes in interest rates with an impact on ESG.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 10 % Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 60 %
literature: Basic: Radová, J., Dvořák, P., & Málek, J. (2013). Finanční matematika pro každého (8th ed.). Grada Publishing. Radová, J. et al. (2011). Finanční matematika pro každého - příklady (2nd ed.). Grada Publishing. Recommended: Cipra, T. (2005). Praktický průvodce finanční a pojistnou matematikou. Ekopress. Macháček, O. (1995). Finanční a pojistná matematika : úrok a úročení, modely opakovaných plateb, burzovní operace při složeném úročení, pojistné operace. Prospektrum.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The students will gain knowledge of the functions and importance of public and private financial institutions in the open economy, they will gain an overview of the structure of active and passive operations of public budgets (state budgets and budgets of local governments), state funds, health insurance companies, central bank, commercial banks and collective investment funds, they will gain specific technical knowledge of the functioning of the financial (including foreign exchange) market and an overview of the factors influencing financial decision-making of these institutions, they will understand the interlinkages between monetary, exchange rate and fiscal policy in an open economy.
course_content: 1) Introduction to the financial economics, money and financial markets (basic terminology, monetary principles and relationships, categorization and parties in the financial markets, investment assets, global financial market). 2) Commercial banks and investment funds (functions of banking system, their balance sheet, categorization of financial services, case study: open position as a factor of risks and profitability on financial markets). 3) Money, bond and stock markets in terms of structure and riskiness (relations between issuer and investor, primary and secondary markets, risks of issuer and investor, types of interest rates, case study: yield curve and expectations theory). 4) Foreign exchange markets (organizational structure of FX markets, FX operations, exchange rate quotations, forward exchange rate and interest rate parity, fundamental determinants of exchange rate changes, case study: currency risk management). 5) Central bank and its role in ensuring monetary and financial stability (functions of central bank, central bank’s balance sheet and policy tools, money emission and monetary aggregates, inflation targeting and quantitative easing, exchange rate policy and FX interventions, micro- and macro-prudential policy, case study: Czech national bank’s loss). 6) Selected issues of financial and monetary macroeconomics (balance of payments and external equilibrium, choice of exchange rate regime, financial crises, inflation and model AS-AD, case study: interest rates and economic cycles). 7) Basic principles of public finance and management in the public sector (causes of public finance development, arguments, functions and principles, public choice theory, public choice in the public sector) 8) Public budget system (State budget, budgets of territorial self-governing units, State funds and Health insurance companies), budgetary rules and budget imbalance (budget deficit, public debt 9) Financial management of territorial self-governing units (revenues, expenditures, budget and budgetary process, fiscal federalism, case study: management of selected cities, SWOT analysis) 10) Public expenditures (theory of public expenditures, analysis of mandatory expenditures at different levels of the budget system of the Czech Republic, possibilities of public expenditure allocation, methods of evaluating the efficiency of public expenditures) 11) Public revenues, taxes and tax system if the Czech Republic (historical and theoretical aspects, tax power, tax determination, case study: income taxes, taxes on consumption, budgetary allocation of taxes, local fees) 12) Social security (case study: possibilities of pension system reform), health care economics (case study: problems of health care in the Czech Republic - financing, population ageing and its impact on health care expenditures), economics of the education system and financing of national defence (military Keynesianism, case study: defence financing in the Czech Republic, expenditure analysis, public procurement in the field of defence and security)
learning_outcomes: - understand the functioning of private and public financial institutions; - compare and analyze the advantages and disadvantages of individual financial instruments; - use specific technical knowledge in the field of financial decision-making at the level of private and public financial institutions; - classify financial risks and choose procedures to hedge them - understand the interrelationships between monetary, exchange rate and fiscal policy.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 50 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: Maaytová, A., Ochrana, F., Pavel, J., Sedmihradská, L., Tepperová, J., & Vítek, L. (2015). Veřejné finance v teorii a praxi. Grada Publishing. Dvořák, P., Kodera, J., Mandel, M., & Revenda, Z. (2023). Peněžní ekonomie a bankovnictví. Ekopress. Recommended: Durčáková, J., & Mandel, M. (2007). Mezinárodní finance. Management Press. Maaytová a kol.: Studijní materiály k 11F201 pro Moodle (2022).

course_title: Financial theory, policy and institutions (in English)
university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: Students will acquire knowledge of public and private financial organizations in a market economy; get acquainted with assets, liabilities and basic operations of the public budget, sovereign funds, central bank and commercial banking system, investment companies; get introduced to the technical processes of financial markets’ functioning and decision-making of financial organizations; understand interconnections between monetary, fiscal and exchange rate policies.
course_content: 1) Introduction to the financial economics, money and financial markets (basic terminology, monetary principles and relationships, categorization and parties in the financial markets, investment assets, global financial market) 2) Commercial banks and investment funds (functions of banking system, their balance sheet, categorization of financial services, case study: open position as a factor of risks and profitability on financial markets). 3) Money, bond and stock markets in terms of structure and riskiness (relations between issuer and investor, primary and secondary markets, risks of issuer and investor, types of interest rates, case study: yield curve and expectations theory). 4) Foreign exchange markets (organizational structure of FX markets, FX operations, exchange rate quotations, forward exchange rate and interest rate parity, fundamental determinants of exchange rate changes, case study: currency risk management). 5) Central bank and its role in ensuring monetary and financial stability (functions of central bank, central bank’s balance sheet and policy tools, money emission and monetary aggregates, inflation targeting and quantitative easing, exchange rate policy and FX interventions, micro- and macro-prudential policy, case study: Czech national bank’s loss). 6) Selected issues of financial and monetary macroeconomics (balance of payments and external equilibrium, choice of exchange rate regime, financial crises, inflation and model AS-AD, case study: interest rates and economic cycles). 7) Principles and basics of public finances, public sector management (development of public finances; arguments, functions and principles, public choice theory and its application in public sector). 8) Budget system, budgetary rules and budgetary disequilibrium (public finance deficit, public debt, fiscal federalism, case study: economic activity of a selected city, SWOT-analysis). 9) Public expenditures (theories of public expenditures, mandatory expenditures on different levels of the public sector in Czech Republic, allocation of public funds, evaluation of the effectiveness of public spending). 10) Public revenues, taxes and tax system in Czech Republic (theoretical and historical aspects, tax authority, tax determination, case study: income tax and consumption tax). 11) Social security and the economics of health system (case study: potential reforms of the pension system; case study: health system in Czech Republic and its issues - funding, population ageing, rising expenditures). 12) Economics of educational system (case study: funding of education in Czech Republic, differences between primary/secondary/tertiary education funding). Funding of national security and defense (military Keynesianism, case study: defense spending in Czech Republic, cost analysis, public procurement in defense and security).
learning_outcomes: - understand the operations of public and commercial financial organizations; - assess the differences between various investment instruments; - apply the technical tools of financial decision-making for public and private financial organizations; - distinguish between different types of financial risks and strategies of their management; - explain the nexus between monetary, exchange rate and fiscal policies.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 50 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: Mishkin, F. S. (2016). The economics of money, banking and financial markets. Pearson. Tresch, R. W. (2008). Public sector economics. Palgrave Macmillan.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course is aimed at presenting accounting transactions in financial statements with the focus on measurement of particular accounting elements. The students will perform the closing of books and will prepare annual financial statements according to the Czech Accounting Standards (balance sheet, income statement, cash flow statement, statement of changes in equity and notes to financial statements), including an annual report.
course_content: 1) The regulation of accounting in the Czech Republic in the context of global harmonization processes. 2) The content and form of financial statements in the CR. 3) Non-current tangible and intangible assets: definition, measurement, repairs and subsequent expenditures, impairment, operational and financial leasing, presentation. 4) Non-current and current financial assets: definition of proprietary and debt instruments, measurement, presentation. 5) Inventory: definition, measurement, methods of evidence of purchased and own production supplies, allowances to inventories, presentation. 6) Non-current and current accounts receivable: allowances to accounts receivable, accepted bill of exchange as a payment method. 7) Non-current and current term liabilities: accounting for partners and employees, accounting for taxes and donations, bills of exchange. 8) Provisions: the nature of provisions, specifics in the CR. 9) Foreign currency translations: daily and fixed exchange rates, measurement and presentation of assets and liabilities in foreign currencies. 10) Share capital: creation; structure; contributions and distributions; retained earnings and their treatment in various forms of business entities. 11) Closing of accounting books: inventory, accruals and deferrals. 12) Annual financial statements: balance sheet, income statement, cash flow statement, statement of changes in equity and notes to financial statements, practical preparation of financial statements, the information potential of financial statements. 13) Issuing of financial statements. 14) Content and form of the annual report.
learning_outcomes: - understand the content annual financial statements in the Czech Republic; - prepare annual financial statements according to the Czech Accounting Standards; - judge the "true and fair view" presented in Czech financial statements.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 14 % Term paper: 10 % Mid-term test(s): 16 % Final test: 60 %
literature: Basic: Randáková, M. et al. (2018). Finanční účetnictví v České republice po 1.1.2016 (podnikatelské subjekty). Oeconomica. Strouhal, J., Židlická, R., & Cardová, Z. (2014). Účetnictví. Velká kniha příkladů. Změny související s NOZ (1st ed.). Albatros Media/BizBooks.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: Students acquire comprehensive knowledge of capital markets in market economy and their organization, covering the bond and stock markets and the activities of institutional investors on the world capital markets. The course focuses on three areas closely associated with capital market. The first topic is the capital market in a market economy, its structure, functions, importance and system, methods and instruments of regulation and supervision. The second area relates to investment instruments trading on the capital markets, their characteristic features and principles of valuation. The third issue focuses on capital markets investors, including its incentives, criterions, activities, position and importance.
course_content: 1. Role of Financial Markets and Institutions in economy. Basic principles, functions, structure and importance of capital markets (savings allocation, capital market characteristics,capital market structure, capital market position on economy). 2. Capital market regulation and supervision (reason for regulation and supervision, methods and instrument of regulation and supervision, regulation and supervision in Czech Republic). 3. Trends on the worlds capital markets (basic developing trend and related conditions, future of the world capital markets). 4. Bond markets. Bond valuation and basic principles of bond analysis. 5. Stock markets. Stock valuation. Basic principles fundamental, technical a psychological stock analyses. Efficient market theory. 6. Financial derivatives markets (options, financial futures, swaps, warrants). New instruments on the capital markets. 7. Other investment instruments markets (precious metals market,real estates market, art collections market). 8. International capital markets and institutional investors (characteristics, investors, instruments, return, risk and liquidity). 9. Primary and secondary markets. Exchange and OTC markets. Exchange system and its components. 10. Prague Stock Exchange. RM-system. 11. World exchange centre (NYSE, LSE, TSE, EURONEXT). 12. Investment banking (definition of investment banking, institutions of investment banking, models of incorporation commercial and investment banking, investment services). 13. Collective investing (conception, advantages and disadvantages of collective investing, institutions of collective investing, measurement of funds performance, collective investing in Czech Republic). 14. Pension funds (pension funds position on the capital markets, types of pension plans, investment policy of pension funds, pension additional insurance in Czech Republic).
learning_outcomes: - understand how capital markets work and what their functions in market economy are; - describe and compare selected world exchanges, including Prague Stock Exchange and RM-System; - calculate return, risk and liquidity of different investment instruments; - valuate stock or bond using elementary methods, procedures and fundamental analysis models; - apply basic methods, tools and principles of technical and psychological analysis on the capital markets; - evaluate activities, position and importance of institutional investors on capital markets.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 20 % Final test: 80 %
literature: Basic: VESELÁ, Jitka. Investování na kapitálových trzích. Praha: Wolters Kluwer Česká republika, 2011. ISBN 978-80-7357-647-9. Veselá, J. (2005). Burzy a burzovní obchody : výchozí texty ke studiu. Oeconomica. VESELÁ, Jitka. Investování na kapitálových trzích. Praha: ASPI, 2007. ISBN 978-80-7357-297-6. Musílek, P. (2011). Trhy cenných papírů. Ekopress. Veselá, J. (2007). Investování na kapitálových trzích v příkladech. Oeconomica. Musílek, P. (2002). Trhy cenných papírů. Ekopress. Recommended: Jílek, J. (2009). Akciové trhy a investování. Grada. Jílek, J. (2009). Finanční trhy a investování. Grada. Polouček, S. (2006). Bankovnictví. Beck.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to combine theoretical knowledge of macroeconomics and economic policy with practical applications in the field of financial market analysis. The course focuses on the understanding of the national economy as an economic system, the interrelationships between the various macroeconomic aggregates, the nature of the creation and use of macroeconomic forecasts and their implications for the various segments of financial markets. Attention will be paid to the links between the situation in financial markets and the emergence, course and consequences of financial and economic crises. Recently, the role and importance of fiscal policy has been increasing, not only in the context of the ageing population and the associated rising government expenditure, but also in the context of the implementation of a stabilisation function within the framework of "post-crisis" stimulation of aggregate demand. Among other things, these factors are leading to an increase in government debt and hence to an increase in the issuance of government securities. The course therefore also examines the repercussions of increased fiscal policy activity on financial markets.
course_content: 1. Interrelationships between macroeconomic and financial market developments 2. Macroeconomic data and macroeconomic analysis (content, structure and interpretation) 3. Use of basic macroeconomic identities in macroeconomic analyses 4. Macroeconomic forecasting and its reflection on financial markets 5. Macroeconomic sources of anomalies and irrationality in financial markets 6. The interrelationship between financial and economic crises 7. The response of financial markets to government policy over the business cycle 8. The ageing population: a comparison of pension systems and their impact on financial markets 9. The impact of tax policy on the real economy and financial markets 10 Public debt financiers: reasons for holding government securities from the perspective of individual entities 11. Capacity and requirements of financial markets to become a creditor of the government sector 12. Impacts and reflections of government debt on international financial markets
learning_outcomes: - Understand the interlinkages between macroeconomic developments, economic policy actions and financial market developments, including their most significant manifestations and anomalies. - Be able to correctly use and interpret macroeconomic data in analyses. - To be familiar with basic macroeconomic aggregates, their interdependence on real data of the Czech economy. Identify sources of possible macroeconomic imbalances. - Find, understand and apply publicly available macroeconomic forecasts (CNB, Ministry of Finance, commercial banks) from the perspective of an economic policy maker, banker, corporation, household and financial market trader. - Understand and discuss the interrelationships between the various segments of financial markets and crisis phenomena of a financial and economic nature. - Understand the effects of fiscal policy and the structure of their revenues and expenditures on financial markets. - Define the specifics of government-issued securities and their manifestations in financial markets. - Discuss the implications of the growing debt burden of the government sector on domestic and international financial markets.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 50 % Final test: 50 %
literature: Basic: VEJMĚLEK, Jan; ŽĎÁREK, Václav; CHALOUPKA, Lubomír; ROJÍČEK, Marek. Makroekonomická analýza - od teorie k aplikaci. 1. vyd. Zlín: Grada Publishing, 2025. 888 p. ISBN 978-80-271-3735-0. Recommended: Titze, M. et al. (2021). Praktické příklady z makroekonomické analýzy (2nd ed.). Nakladatelství Oeconomica. https://oeconomica.vse.cz/wp-content/uploads/publication/21470/Titze_Prakticke-priklady-z-makroekonomicke-analyzy.pdf Lipschitz, L., & Schadler, S. (2019). Macroeconomics for professionals : a guide for analysts and those who need to understand them. Cambridge University Press. Abel, A. B., Bernanke, B., & Croushore, D. D. (2014). Macroeconomics. Pearson. Pošta, V. (2018). Makroekonomická analýza na příkladu české ekonomiky (1st ed.). C. H. Beck. Hindriks, J., & Myles, G. D. (2006). Intermediate public economics. MIT Press. university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to develop accounting information system relevant for financial management of company operating activities and to define the aims and contents of product-oriented and responsibility accounting including their mutual relations.
course_content: 1. Relation between financial, cost and management accounting - reasons for differences in the content, details and extend of accounting information - classification of organizational activities according to their contribution to profit achievement - major activity - product creation process - role and functions of controller (managerial accounting) in a company 2. Business performance measurement and management - criteria for performance management following shareholders’ perspective - criteria for business performance management following managers’ perspective with emphasis on product creation process - criteria for costs and profit control - cost concepts in cost accounting - significance of differences between cost/expense concepts in financial and managerial accounting 3. Cost classification for financial management - cost classification by nature, advantages, constraints, areas of use - cost classification into prime and overhead - use of costing in operational and tactical management of prime costs - cost classification into variable and fixed, generally theoretical context, use in management - cost classification into direct and indirect costs 4. Basic relationships between volume, cost and profit (CVP) - basic parameters of profit management, theirs definition - methods for recognizing variable and fixed costs - used and unused fixed costs - determining the volume of the break-even point and the required profit - setting the maximum amount of variable and fixed costs - determination of the selling price - fixed and unused fixed costs 5. Standard costing and variance analysis - role and functions of variance analysis - settings of standards - measurement of actual values and methods of variance measurement - profit variance analysis in a simple product - profit variance in a product mix situation - input-mix-variance 6. Recognition of product creation process in accounting - profit and loss statements with different cost classification - impact of inventories’ valuation on profit measurement - measurement of profit of non-costumized and costumized products/services - managerial profit and loss statements 7. Costing for business management - costing and its application in basic decision making - absorption costing, content, use in management - variable costing, content, use in management 8. Costing terminology - definition of the subject of allocation - content and structure of cost card - cost allocation methods and principles - step methods of allocation 9. Recognition of financial relationships among responsibility centers, revenue concept in cost accounting - recognition of relationships among responsibility centers on actual-costs basis - recognition of relationships among responsibility centers on standard-costs basis - cost assignment according to origin and responsibility, internal costs - relationship of profit measurement at corporate and responsibility centers’ level 10. Role of cost budgets in planning and budgeting system - prerequisites for the effective functioning of plans and budgets in an unstable market environment - aims and functions of budgeting - organization of budgeting process - stage in budgeting of profit 11. Budgets of cost centers and budgetary control - structure of costs in budgets of cost centers - relation among budgeting, costing and managerial profit and loss statements - methods for budgeting of overhead costs - budgetary control (fixed, flexible and flexed budget) - trade-offs between profit structure of the company and budgetary control approach 12. Responsibility accounting - responsibility accounting as part of accountability management, causes of its development - preconditions of efficient application of accountability management - organizational structure of the company - centralization and decentralization in accountability management - economic structure of the company and types of responsibility centers 13. Performance measurement of responsibility centers - the cost of responsibility centers - the revenues of responsibility centers - transfer pricing types - economic result of center
learning_outcomes: - explain the differences in substance and management accounting system design for product costing and compare it with the accounting information for incentive of managers at different levels of management; - choose the appropriate structure of product costs and benefits depending on decision-making tasks; - propose the design on management accounting system; recommend its changes including basis for conclusions; - develop the management reports for measuring and evaluating performance of the company and its responsibility centres; - apply the management accounting terminology appropriately.
assessment_methods: Active lecture/seminar/workshop/tutorial participation: 30 % Final test: 70 %
literature: Basic: FIBÍROVÁ, Jana; PETERA, Petr; WAGNER, Jaroslav; ŠOLJAKOVÁ, Libuše. Manažerské účetnictví : nástroje a metody. Praha: Wolters Kluwer ČR, 2019. ISBN 978-80-7598-486-9. Recommended: Fibírová, J., Wagner, J., & Šoljaková, L. (2017). Manažerské účetnictví I. : případové studie a příklady (pro kurzy 1MU305 a 1MU211). Oeconomica, nakladatelství VŠE. Fibírová, J., Král, B., Matyáš, O., Menšík, M., Petera, P., Wagner, J., Zralý, M., & Šoljaková, L. (2018). Manažerské účetnictví. Management Press.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim is to give basic information concerning fundamental notions and methods of linear algebra, differential and integral calculus and the basic methods of solution of differential equations. These notions and methods are necessary for economical courses.
course_content: 1) Arithmetical vectors and algebraic operations with them (linear combination, linear dependance and linear independance, scalar product). 2) Matrices, rank of matrix. Systems of linear equations, Frobenius theorem. 3) Multiplication of matrices, regular matrix, inverse matrix, matrix equations, determinants, Cramer's rule. 4) Limits of sequences and limits of real functions of one real variable. 5) Continuity of real functions of one real variable. 6) Derivatives of real functions of one real variable, higher order derivatives, L'Hospital's rule. 7) Increasing and decreasing functions, increasing-decreasing test, critical points test, the first derivative test and the second derivative test for graphing and maximum-minimum problems, the second derivative and concavity, the second derivative test for concavity, inflection points, drawing graphs,Taylor polynomial. 8) Real functions of two real variables, partial derivatives, partial derivatives of second order, smooth functions of first and second order, local maxima and local minima, constraint maxima and minima, method of Jacobi's determinant, method of Lagrange multipliers. 9) Indefinite integral, integration by parts and the substitution method, integral of rational functions. 10) Definite integral, improper integral. 11) Differential equations (first and second order differential equations with constant coefficients and special right side). 12) Reserve.
learning_outcomes: After the course student will be able to: 1) solve systems of linear equations; 2) compute inverse matrix, or determine whether inverse matrix exists; 3) solve simple matrix equations with the use of inverse matrices; 4) compute determinant of matrix and use Cramer's rule; 5) compute derivatives of elementary functions, use L'Hospital's rule, use first and second derivative tests, draw graphs; 6) determine local and constraint maxima and minima of real functions of two real variables; 7) use integration method by parts and substitution method; 8) use Newton's definition of definite integral and definition of improper integral.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 20 % Final test: 40 % Final oral exam: 30 % Homework during the semester: 10 %
literature: Basic: Klůfa, J., & Pasáčková, J. (2023). Učebnice matematiky (1) pro studenty VŠE. Ekopress.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course explains the functions of a central bank both in a market and a transition economy (with focus on the Czech Republic). Student obtain the knowledge of selected issues in monetary economics - monetary policy, monetary theory and history, and banking regulation and supervision.
course_content: 1. Money and the economy (issuing and history of money, theoretical and empirical definition of money, monetary aggregates). 2. Central bank (establishment, functions, balance). 3. Instruments of monetary policy (open market operations, discount policy, reserve requirements, foreign exchange intervention). 4. Efficiency of monetary policy (goals, time lags, central bank independence, international effects). 5. Banking regulation and supervision. 6. Financial stability. 7. Banking system of the Czech Republic and the EMU.
learning_outcomes: - understand issuing and circulation of money in the economy, monetary policy, and banking regulation and supervision.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 70 %
literature: Basic: Revenda, Z. (2011). Centrální bankovnictví. Management Press. ROJÍČEK, Marek; SPĚVÁČEK, Vojtěch; VEJMĚLEK, Jan; ZAMRAZILOVÁ, Eva; ŽĎÁREK, Václav. Makroekonomická analýza: teorie a praxe. 1. vyd. Praha: Grada Publishing, 2016. 543 p. ISBN 978-80-247-5858-9. Mishkin, F. S. (2022). Economics of money, banking, and financial markets. Pearson. Recommended: JONES, CH.: Macroeconomics, Wiley, 2025. ISBN: 978-1-324-06368-1 (vybrané kapitoly) MANKIW, G. Macroeconomics, The 12th edition, Macmillan Learning, 2025.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The students acquire a comprehensive knowledge of the forms and motivations associated with the movement of international capital and the functioning of the foreign exchange market. They will be acquainted with the macroeconomic and microeconomic aspects of the exchange rate movement. They acquire specific technical knowledge and basic skills in foreign exchange market operations - foreign exchange arbitrage, speculation and hedging. Students will become familiar with the role of central banks in the foreign exchange market (problems of foreign exchange interventions and exchange rate arrangements. The seminars are focused on the work with the balance of payments report, the possibility of prediction of course development, and examples of hedging, arbitrage and speculation in individual types of foreign exchange operations.
course_content: 1. International Financial Transactions and Balance of Payments 2. Introduction to the foreign exchange market 3. Exchange rates and foreign exchange intervention systems of the central bank 4. Fundamental analysis of the exchange rate (purchasing power parity, interest rate parity, balance of payments approach) 5. Technical analysis of the exchange rate (basic terms, technical indicators) 6. Spot, forward and swap foreign exchange operations (hedging, arbitrage and speculation) 7. Currency futures and currency options (use for hedging, arbitrage and speculation) 8. Foreign exchange position and transaction foreign exchange exposure 9. Economic and translation (accounting) foreign exchange exposure 10. Foreign direct investment (macroeconomic and microeconomic approach) 11. Country rating and sovereign risk (golden rules of rating, long-term and short-term rating) 12. Portfolio investment in international context(domestic and international portfolio, systematic risk) 13. Foreign bonds and eurobonds market (analysis from the point of view of the issuer and the investor)
learning_outcomes: - understand the mechanism of foreign exchange markets as well as how exchange rates are quoted; - understand the techniques of the main foreign exchange transactions; - describe the factors that drive exchange rates, in particular how spot and forward rates are related to interest rates and inflation dynamics and how to use these relations in foreign exchange rates forecasting; - identify the nature of foreign exchange risk and types of foreign exchange exposure that company faces, including using of these techniques to hedge foreign exchange risk; - identify the most important changes in international financial environment since the end of Bretton Woods.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 30 % Final test: 70 %
literature: Basic: Durčáková, J., & Mandel, M. (2020). Mezinárodní finance a devizový trh. Ekopress. Eiteman, D. K., Moffett, M. H., & Stonehill, A. I. (2021). Multinational business finance. Pearson. Recommended: FOX, Roland; MADURA, Jeff. International financial management. Andover: South-Western/Cengage Learning, 2011. ISBN 978-1-4080-3229-9.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The course introduces the economic principles of insurance compared to other financial instruments. The students are acquainted with the basic principles of activity of insurance companies as financial institutions including the main characteristics of the insurance activity. Students are also familiarized with the structure of insurance industry and its regulation, with the structure of insurance market and its main development tendencies.
course_content: 1. Risk and impact of contingency on people, their economic activity, quantification of Risks, segmentation of Risks, dimensions of Risks. 2. Risk Management, its stages, and importance of Risk Management. 3. Insurance as instrument of elimination of Risks, Insurance as a financial category. 4. Macroeconomic and microeconomic importance of Insurance, classification of Insurance. 5. Insurable risks in commercial insurance and dimensions of commercial insurance. 6. Insurance forms and methods of determination of insurance benefit. 7. Insurance premium, basic methods of calculation and policy rating. 8. Technical Reserves, its importance in the insurance activity, types of Technical Reserves, principles of investment of Technical Reserves. 9. Reinsurance, importance of reinsurance in the insurance activity, types and methods of reinsurance, alternative methods of risk partition. 10. Risk of the insurance company, especially the risk of underwriting. 11. Specifics in the economy of insurance companies, solvency (Solvency II). 12. Life insurance, basic characteristics of the main products. 13. Basic methods for premium determination in life insurance. 14. Non-life insurance, basic characteristics of the main products - non-life personal. insurance, property, financial losses, liability. 15. Pension insurance, construction of pension insurance. 16. Basic methods for premium determination in non-life insurance. 17. Insurance industry and its position in the economy, insurance companies, insurance brokers and other institution in the insurance industry. 18. Regulation of the insurance industry, its importance, methods of regulation, legislation, state supervision over the insurance industry. 19. Insurance market: insurance penetration indicators, insurance market in the Czech Republic, world insurance market, factors influencing on the insurance market.
learning_outcomes: - understand the functioning of insurance like a financial category; - use the insurance as an instrument solving the consequences of randomness; - utilize several forms and types of insurance; - understand the basic characteristics of insurance - insurance benefits, policy liabilities, reinsurance, solvency of insurance companies; - understand the differences between life and non-life insurance; - understand the variances of particular forms of non-life insurance (property insurance, liability insurance, credit insurance, etc.); - apply basic methods of determination of insurance benefits; - apply basic approaches to determination of premiums in life and non-life insurance; - understand the main factors impacting insurance markets.
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 20 % Final test: 80 %
literature: Basic: Ducháčková, E. (2025). Pojištění a pojišťovnictví. Ekopress. Majtánová, A. (2006). Poisťovníctvo : teória a prax = Teorie a praxe pojišťovnictví. Ekopress. Recommended: VOSTATEK, J. Sociální a soukromé pojištění. 2. vyd. Praha : ASPI Publishing, 2002. ISBN 8085963213. ČEJKOVÁ, Viktória. Pojistný trh. Praha: Grada, 2002. ISBN 80-247-0137-5.

university: Prague University of Economics and Business
aims: The aim of the course is to obtain knowledge and skills in the understanding of the legal system in Czech republic and its principles with an emphasis on contract law, entrepreneurial law, corporation law and commercial dispute resolution law.
course_content: 1.Introduction to law (allowance 10/0) a. Concept of Law, Sources of international, EU and national Law, Legal systems b. EU institution, EU law; EU internal market c. Legal system in Czech Republic; Sources of private and Commercial law; Czech Constitutional Law 2.Legal person (allowance 12/0) a. Legal persons; natural person and juristic person; Legal requirement of enterprise; Subjects of business activity b. Corporations c. Legal conduct; Representation d. Public registrar - commercial; insolvency; enterprises 3.Property law (allowance 8/0) a. Tangible and intangible things; movable and immovable things; property law; common property law b. Securities; Intellectual property c. Cadastre of Real Estates; Public registrar held by Notary Chambers; Intellectual property databases 4.Obligation (allowance 12/0) a. Types of obligations; creation; security; termination b. Obligations from infringement of law (liability for damage, defects and delays) c. Purchase contract; Contract for work; Mandate agreement d. Lease agreement; Licence agreement; Loan agreement; Credit agreement e. Employment agreement 5.Commercial disputes resolution (allowance 6/0) a. Court proceeding; jurisdiction; Evidence; Appellation and Extraordinary Writs; Statute of Limitations b. Alternative dispute resolution; Arbitration and Mediation
learning_outcomes: Having successfully completed the course, students will be able to - define the basic terms in the field of theory of law - identify the applicable legal provisions governing the status of businessmen and their mutual relations - determine the differences between individual types of business companies - identify the conditions for pursuing business in the Czech Republic - know the basic types of contracts concluded between businessmen and characterize the basic rights and obligations of the parties - identify the conditions for liability and the consequences of breaching obligations - specify the conditions for different means of dispute resolution before court as well as ADR
assessment_methods: Mid-term test(s): 25 % Final oral exam: 60 % Home preparation and Moodle: 15 %
